Lauréate du prix « Winner », la Kia PV5 met en avant la stratégie visionnaire Platform Beyond Vehicle (PBV) de Kia ainsi que son approche modulaire du design. Elle adopte une face avant commune associée à des modules arrière flexibles pouvant être adaptés à différents usages. La gamme comprend des versions Passenger, Cargo, WAV (Wheelchair Accessible Vehicle) et Crew, chacune conçue pour offrir un espace optimisé, une grande praticité et une excellente fonctionnalité, tout en conservant une identité stylistique cohérente. Cette nouvelle distinction vient enrichir le palmarès grandissant du PV5, après l'obtention récente d'un Gold Award lors des iF Design Awards 2026. Le succès continu de Kia aux Red Dot Award Les performances constantes de Kia dans les grandes compétitions internationales de design témoignent de la solidité de sa stratégie design à long terme. Les modèles Kia ayant remporté le titre « Best of the Best » aux Red Dot Award sont désormais : EV6 (2022), EV9 (2024), EV3 (2025), EV4 (2026). Qu'est-ce qui fait du MobED de HMG un lauréat des Red Dot Award ? Développé par le Robotics LAB du groupe constructeur, MobED (Mobile Eccentric Droid) est une plateforme robotique mobile conçue pour évoluer sur différents terrains et dans de multiples environnements d'utilisation. MobED incarne la philosophie de design « Refined Edge » du Robotics LAB, une approche visant à améliorer continuellement l'intégration des robots dans le quotidien humain. Cette identité stylistique façonne non seulement son apparence distinctive, mais également ses interactions, afin d'inspirer confiance et proximité dans des espaces partagés.

title: Winners of Nedbank N*ovation Data and Analytics Masters Challenge announced
summary: Nedbank announced winners of its 2026 Data and Analytics Masters Challenge in Johannesburg, with prize money of ZAR500,000 and fast-tracked recruitment for top performers.
content: South Africa's Nedbank has announced the winners of the Nedbank N*ovation Data and Analytics Masters Challenge 2026, following weeks of rigorous virtual competition and an in-person finale in Johannesburg. The national Data and Analytics Challenge is designed to identify and recruit South Africa's most advanced data and analytics practitioners, and this year's edition culminated in a live contest where finalists were tested on their ability to solve complex, real-world banking problems. The winner of the data engineering track was Mogale Thobejane, with Tshifhiwa Gift Moila and Tinashe Chinyati coming in second and third place. The data science and machine learning track was won by Edward Rycroft, with Alex Jacobs in second and Vusani Khosa in third. Contestants shared ZAR500,000 (US$30,000) in prize money. The top performers will benefit from a fast-tracked recruitment process, highlighting the bank's ongoing investment in building future-ready data and analytics capabilities. In collaboration with partners including OfferZen, Zindi, Otinga, and Microsoft, the challenge potentially connects top talent directly with career opportunities. "This challenge is about more than competition, it reflects our belief that data excellence is a strategic capability, and this challenge was a novel one with a positive outcome demonstrated by the level of quality on display at the challenge," said Nedbank Group technology executive Thando Lukhele.

title: AI startups invited to attend ClawCon Nairobi
summary: African AI startups are invited to attend ClawCon Nairobi, a free community-led event focused on personal AI, agents, workflows, and OpenClaw tools, taking place on June 18 at Strathmore University.
content: African startups are invited to attend ClawCon Nairobi, a free, community-led gathering focused on personal AI, agents, workflows, OpenClaw, and the emerging tools that let individuals build more powerful digital systems for themselves. Set for June 18 at Strathmore University, ClawCon is designed for builders, founders, developers, creators, operators, researchers, students, and AI-curious people. It will feature live demos, founder presentations, Q&As, and conversations around what personal AI could mean for Nairobi's tech ecosystem. The event was born from the OpenClaw community, and its mission to put power back into people's hands - giving individuals more sovereignty over personal AI, their workflows, their tools, and their digital lives.

title: Kenya's Fikra launches AI inference API for African developers, startups, businesses
summary: Kenyan startup Fikra API launches an OpenAI-compatible AI inference API tailored for African developers with M-Pesa payments, local currency pricing, and flat-rate token pricing of $1 per 2 million tokens.
content: Kenya's Fikra API has launched an AI inference API built specifically for African developers, startups, and businesses. Founded by James Miano earlier this year, Fikra API is an OpenAI-compatible inference API targeting African developers, with M-Pesa payments built in and pricing in local terms rather than USD. Developers can send requests to Fikra's endpoint and get responses from large language models, the same way they would with OpenAI or Anthropic, but with pricing, payment methods, and token economics designed for African markets. "If you are a developer or startup founder in Nairobi, Lagos, or Cape Town and you want to build an AI-powered product, the standard path is OpenAI or Anthropic. Both require a minimum spend of around US$20 before you can access the API," Miano told Disrupt Africa. "That figure sounds small, but in Nairobi US$20 is close to a week's salary for many people. For those who can afford it, the problem does not stop there, a frontier model output pricing is expensive and burns through tokens quickly, forcing constant top-ups that compound the cost." The second barrier is payment. "Most people in Africa are not cardholders. OpenAI and Anthropic require a credit or debit card. Mobile money is how the majority of people here actually transact, and none of the major AI API providers support it," Miano said. Fikra API exists to solve both. Fully-bootstrapped, the startup onboarded its first 10 users within days of launch. "We have also shipped two proprietary models - a custom embeddings model ready for inference, and Fikra Nano 1B which is a ternary weight fine-tuned model we built in-house. The ternary weight pipeline is a genuinely uncommon capability and gives us a technical foundation most API providers at this stage do not have," said Miano. Currently only active in Kenya, with payment infrastructure live via Paystack which covers much of sub-Saharan Africa, Fikra's model is built to scale continent-wide. "Any African developer with a smartphone and mobile money access is the target user," said Miano. The startup charges US$1 per two million tokens. "We do not split input and output pricing the way most providers do, it is one flat rate. Payment is via Paystack, which supports M-Pesa and mobile money across multiple African markets. We are pre-revenue at this stage with the commercial push beginning at launch," said Miano. "The main challenge is onboarding friction, getting a developer from signup to their first successful API call as fast as possible, because if that moment takes too long you lose them. We identified this early and have been iterating on it. The broader difficulty is distribution. The developers we are building for are not always in the same communities where tech press reaches, so finding and activating them requires ground-level work."

title: Holocene closes Southern Africa's first dedicated climate-tech fund
summary: VC firm Holocene announced the final close of Holocene Ventures Fund I (HVF1), Southern Africa's first dedicated high-growth climate tech fund. The fund has backed 10 companies in 18 months, creating over 500 jobs, achieving a 2x markup on invested capital, and attracting $8 in follow-on capital for every $1 invested. Portfolio companies include FARO, ScootHero, and Yongeza, each receiving at least 12 months of hands-on operational support.
content: VC firm Holocene has announced the final close of Holocene Ventures Fund I (HVF1), Southern Africa's first dedicated high-growth climate tech fund. Holocene targets promising local founders at the earliest stages who lack capital and hands-on operational support to scale, especially in a nascent sector such as climate-tech. Through HVF1, it has backed 10 companies in the 18 months it has been deployed, creating over 500 jobs and achieving a 2x markup on invested capital, while attracting US$8 in follow-on capital for every US$1 it invested. "It's clear we need to dramatically accelerate the pace of climate innovation in Africa," founder Josh Romisher said. "The continent will double in size, urbanise, and begin to truly consume during our lifetimes. That can be seen as an impending climate catastrophe or a massive innovation opportunity. We choose to view it as the latter." Portfolio ventures include FARO, a circular economy company built around technology-enabled supply chains for unused inventory; ScootHero, which is deploying over 500 electric motorbikes and more than 50 battery-swap stations across South Africa; and Yongeza, an e-mobility infrastructure company in Uganda. Each investment comes with at least 12 months of embedded, high-touch support from a team of globally trained specialists: operators, executives-in-residence, and experts in revenue growth, fundraising, and grant writing. "What excites me most is not just that Holocene backed us early, it's that they got in the trenches with us," said Wahlied Cole, founder and CEO of ScootHero. "When we needed help with go-to-market, they brought in operators. When we were hitting a ceiling on capital, they helped us unlock grants and asset finance. It wasn't just capital. It was a partnership built on real understanding of what African founders need." Jonathan Smit, exited founder and HVF1 investor, said he invested in Holocene because he believes climate innovation will be one of the defining challenges and opportunities of our generation, particularly in Africa. "What stood out to me was not just the investment thesis, but the quality of the team and the hands-on way they support founders. Holocene is helping build the ecosystem and companies that can deliver meaningful climate impact while creating lasting economic value," he said. The final close marks the beginning of the fund's value-creation phase - the work of driving portfolio companies towards scale and exits. "We've shown that the intersection of climate impact and financial returns is real," Romisher said. "The task now is to scale that proof. We live the Stanford motto: change lives, change organisations, and change the world. That's not always easy, but it is deeply meaningful."

title: How Nigeria's Sokofa connects diaspora buyers with African artisans, small vendors
summary: Nigerian startup Sokofa operates a marketplace linking diaspora buyers in Europe and North America with verified African artisans and small vendors across fashion, art, and beauty. The platform offers made-to-measure ordering with up to 16 custom measurements, an AI virtual try-on tool generating previews in under 25 seconds, and vendor verification. Founded in late 2025, Sokofa is self-funded and aims to put African artisans on the global e-commerce map on their own terms.
content: Nigerian startup Sokofa is a marketplace connecting diaspora buyers across Europe and North America with verified African artisans and small vendors across fashion, art, and beauty. Founded late last year, Sokofa provides access to fashion listings that let a buyer enter their own measurements directly on the product page, up to 16 specific fields depending on the garment, and have the piece cut by hand by the actual vendor, not a factory running standard sizes. The same listings carry an AI virtual try-on tool, where a buyer uploads a photo and gets a generated preview of the outfit on them in under 25 seconds, with the photo deleted after 24 hours. "The vendors are independent artisans currently based in Africa, selling Ankara sets, Adire chords, kaftans, and traditional native wear, verified by Sokofa before they are allowed to list. It is a small, real example of an African startup solving the fit and trust problem that diaspora shoppers have dealt with for years, using a mix of straightforward custom order infrastructure and AI rather than either alone," founder Tope Abuloye told Disrupt Africa. At its core, Abuloye said Sokofa is about putting Africa on the map in global e-commerce "on its own terms". "Diaspora buyers wanting authentic African fashion, art, and beauty have had two weak options: generic marketplaces with no fit guarantee or vendor verification, or relying on word of mouth tailors back home with long lead times and no buyer protection," said Abuloye. "We wanted African artisans to be discoverable and trusted by a global audience the same way any other category of maker is, not treated as a niche or an afterthought. Sokofa brings vendor verification, made to measure ordering, and AI fit preview into one checkout flow, a combination we haven't seen elsewhere in this category." Sokofa is self-funded, built on personal savings and support from family and friends, and has already listed multiple vendors, and made a number of sales. Vendors are based across Africa, while buyers are across Europe and North America. "We're actively expanding vendor recruitment to include Africans in the diaspora who want to sell African-inspired products themselves, several have already expressed interest in listing on the platform even while based outside the continent," Abuloye said. Sokofa runs on a commission model, taking a percentage of each sale.

title: Catalyst Fund's $30m 2nd close deepens bet on Africa's climate resilience founders
summary: Pan-African venture fund Catalyst Fund announced a $30 million second close, bringing total capital closer to its target. The fund backs pre-seed to Series A startups building climate resilience solutions for underserved communities. New investors include IFC, FASA, Shell Foundation, Trafigura Foundation, Speedinvest, BlinkCV, and high-net-worth individuals, joining earlier backers FSD Africa and Cisco Foundation. Catalyst Fund expects final close later in 2026.
content: Catalyst Fund, the pan-African venture fund and venture builder backing founders from pre-seed to Series A, has announced a US$30 million second close, bringing it closer to its target and adding fresh momentum to its mission of strengthening climate resilience across the continent. Catalyst Fund is a pre-seed VC fund and accelerator backing high-impact tech startups that seek to improve the resilience of underserved, climate-vulnerable communities. It partners with mission-driven founders that share our vision of a world where every individual has the tools and opportunities they need to thrive. Until 2023, the organisation offered grant capital to selected startups, but it has since switched to an equity-based model. Its first close of that fund was in September 2023, and the second close brings together IFC, FASA, Shell Foundation, Trafigura Foundation, Speedinvest, BlinkCV, and a group of high-net-worth individuals, joining early backers FSD Africa, Cisco Foundation, and others who validated the thesis at first close. Catalyst Fund expects to reach final close later this year. "Climate adaptation is one of the defining investment themes of the next decade, especially in Africa, where the need is immediate, and the entrepreneurial talent is extraordinary. This second close allows us to double down on our mission: backing ambitious founders building practical, scalable solutions for a climate-changed world, and supporting them not just with capital, but with the hands-on venture-building support they need to grow," said Maelis Carraro, founder and general partner at Catalyst Fund.

title: African Payment Providers Bypass SWIFT for Stablecoin Settlement
summary: African payment service providers (PSPs) are increasingly adopting stablecoin settlement via USDC and USDT to bypass SWIFT's slow, costly correspondent banking system. Swiss-based institutional OTC desk FinchTrade facilitates this shift, offering same-day settlement, reduced FX exposure, and predictable fees. The article highlights a $1M Nigeria-to-Germany payment costing $5,000-$8,000 via stablecoins versus $40,000-$70,000 via SWIFT, with settlement compressed from days to hours. Regulatory frameworks in Nigeria and South Africa are evolving to support this infrastructure, with expansion expected to more African corridors.
content: For African PSPs and EMIs handling cross-border B2B settlement, correspondent banking has become a structural drag. SWIFT settlement windows run T+3 to T+5. Cumulative fees hit 3% to 7% across intermediary banks. Working capital sits tied up in days-long FX exposure. These are now line items PSPs measure against alternatives that didn't exist 18 months ago. FinchTrade, a Swiss-based institutional OTC desk, sees this shift daily. The company serves African payment companies as well as other international flows where stablecoin settlement through USDC and USDT has become an operational default. "For our PSP clients, this is no longer about offering crypto products to end users," says Nicola Boldrini, growth lead at FinchTrade. "It's about whether their cross-border settlement actually settles the same day or takes a week." Three operational realities are driving the migration: settlement reliability, FX exposure compression, and cost predictability. A Nigerian importer paying a German supplier $1 million faces compounding differences: SWIFT settlement typically costs $40,000 to $70,000 with four to five days of FX volatility, while the same payment routed through Naira-to-USDC-to-Euro clears within hours at $5,000 to $8,000 total cost. FinchTrade provides the crypto-fiat conversion layer and connects African PSPs to global stablecoin liquidity, using regulated stablecoin issuers, institutional custody (Fireblocks), and compliance platforms. FinchTrade holds a Swiss license through VQF and has processed over $1 billion in client trading volume since launch, running corridor settlement across Nigerian naira, Ghanaian cedi, Kenyan shilling, and South African rand. The business impact includes settlement time compressed from days to hours, all-in costs reduced by an order of magnitude, and working capital released from in-flight FX exposure. The shift also opens corridors that correspondent banking has historically underserved. Regulatory frameworks are maturing: the Central Bank of Nigeria has indicated openness to stablecoin payment infrastructure in B2B contexts, and South Africa's SARB framework provides workable guidelines. Additional corridors positioned to follow include Tanzania, Uganda, Senegal, and Côte d'Ivoire. Risks remain, including stablecoin issuer concentration.

title: Egypt's BrainsMingle raises $400k seed funding
summary: Egyptian AI-driven professional network BrainsMingle has secured $400,000 in seed funding from BasharSoft Group, Egypt's leading human capital technology group operating WUZZUF and Forasna.
content: Egypt's BrainsMingle, an AI-driven, video-first professional network designed to prioritise authentic human connection, has raised US$400,000 in seed funding. Founded by Belal Amin and Yousef Gamal, BrainsMingle allows experts to deliver live sessions, build thriving communities, manage bookings, and earn from their expertise while ambitious professionals discover world-class knowledge, engage with experts across the globe, and connect with peers who will shape their careers. BrainsMingle, which has already attracted professionals and communities from more than 90 countries, has announced a US$400,000 strategic seed investment from BasharSoft Group, Egypt's leading human capital technology group, which operates WUZZUF and Forasna. This partnership represents BasharSoft's first strategic investment since its acquisition of iCareer last year. BasharSoft Group, which serves over 9 million users through its flagship platforms WUZZUF and Forasna, as well as iCareer and Recruitera, is betting on BrainsMingle to lead the next generation of professional engagement and knowledge-sharing technologies. "We built BrainsMingle around one belief: the most valuable professional moments happen between people, not between people and content. The world does not need another feed to scroll or another profile to maintain. It needs a home, a single place where an ambitious professional can show up, share what they know, and build a community around it," Amin said. "BrainsMingle was never built for one country or one region. It was built for every professional on the planet who has real expertise to share and no single place worthy of it. BasharSoft's investment is a powerful validation of that vision and a commitment to building it the right way." Ameer Sherif, executive chairman of BasharSoft, said his company has been connecting talent to opportunity for 15 years. "We identified a significant gap in how knowledge and experience are shared online. BrainsMingle has built the technology necessary to fill this gap, replacing multiple global tools with a single, sophisticated network that resonates with our mission to empower 50 million people in their careers by 2030," he said.

title: Mondial-2026 : chaleur extrême attendue cette semaine pour joueurs et supporters
summary: Une vague de chaleur extrême, due à un « dôme de chaleur », s'abat sur le centre et l'est des États-Unis ainsi que sur certaines régions du Canada, coïncidant avec le début de la phase à élimination directe de la Coupe du monde 2026. Des températures dangereusement élevées sont attendues jusqu'au week-end, y compris lors des matchs en soirée. La FIFA a prévu des pauses d'hydratation obligatoires, mais des inquiétudes persistent pour la sécurité des joueurs et des supporters.
content: NEW YORK (Etats-Unis), 30 juin 2026 (TAP) - Une chaleur extrême attendra les supporters et les joueurs cette semaine lors de la Coupe du monde, alors qu'un « dôme de chaleur » s'installe sur le centre et l'est des États-Unis - ainsi que sur certaines régions du Canada - en parallèle au lancement de la phase à élimination directe du tournoi. Ce phénomène météorologique - une vaste zone de haute pression qui retient la chaleur et l'humidité - pourrait provoquer des températures dangereusement élevées dans certaines régions du Midwest et de la côte Est américaine. De telles conditions devraient perdurer tout au long du week-end prochain, de Toronto à Kansas City en passant par East Rutherford (New Jersey) et Philadelphie. « Même après le coucher du soleil, il fera encore très chaud », a déclaré Alan Reppert, météorologue chez AccuWeather. « Nous sommes dans une configuration qui va vraiment apporter de la chaleur pendant une bonne partie de l'après-midi et même jusque dans la soirée. » A New York, où un huitième de finale est prévu le 5 juillet, des températures à un pic inédit depuis 2013 sont attendus, a dit Reppert, précisant que même les matchs disputés en soirée pourraient être affectés. « Le soleil sera couché. Cela contribuera à améliorer la situation, mais il fera tout de même très chaud. » Ces conditions pourraient raviver les débats sur la sécurité et le bien-être des joueurs, déjà soulevé il y a un an lors de la Coupe du monde des clubs organisée aux Etats-Unis, le syndicat mondial des joueurs déclarant à l'époque que la canicule constatée devait servir de « signal d'alarme ». La FIFA a instauré pour cette Coupe du monde 2026 des pauses d'hydratation obligatoires de trois minutes lors de chaque mi-temps, une mesure jugée par ses partisans comme bénéfique pour le bien-être des joueurs mais dont les détracteurs estiment qu'elle perturbe le déroulement du jeu. L'instance dirigeante du football mondial n'a pas répondu dans l'immédiat à une demande de commentaire concernant d'éventuelles mesures supplémentaires qui pourraient être prises pour assurer la sécurité des joueurs ou des supporters pendant la période de forte chaleur prévue. Trois des stades accueillant des matchs cette semaine - Atlanta, Dallas et Houston - sont équipés de toits rétractables et de climatisation, ce qui apporte un soulagement considérable. Mais même une simple promenade jusqu'au stade ultramoderne des Dallas Cowboys peut s'apparenter à marcher sur du feu, alors que la ville texane, avec ses vastes étendues de béton et d'asphalte, a été conçue pour les voitures et est particulièrement propice à l'accumulation de chaleur.

title: Cascador invests over $5m in 7 Nigerian tech startups
summary: Africa-focused platform Cascador deployed over $5 million across seven Nigerian startups at its second annual pitch day, including debt and equity investments in Agriarche, Koolboks, Powerstove, First Electric, Fortics, Stears, and Indigenius AI.
content: Cascador, an Africa-focused platform for growth-stage founders building businesses with social impact, has deployed over US$5 million into seven innovative startups at its second annual pitch day. Disrupt Africa reported last March that Cascador had announced the launch of its Catalytic Fund, a US$2 million annual initiative providing tailored financial support to Cascador alumni companies. Since its founding in 2019, Cascador has supported over 60 entrepreneurs who have collectively raised more than US$55 million in capital. The company hosted its second annual on June 3, where over US$5 million of growth capital was deployed to seven innovative African entrepreneurs, who pitched their vision and growth strategies before an influential audience of business leaders and a distinguished panel of judges. The funding recipients included Agriarche, which secured US$1.7 million in debt funding; Koolboks, which banked US$1.4 million in debt; Powerstove, which secured debt funding worth US$1.2 million; and First Electric, which raised US$357,000 in debt. The other funded ventures were Fortics, which raised US$142,000 in debt; Stears, which banked US$450,000 in equity funding; and Indigenius AI, which raised US$250,000 in equity investment. "In just two years, Pitch Day has awarded more than US$9 million to growth-stage African founders, helping to build a new generation of entrepreneurs equipped to scale transformative businesses. We're now looking for the next cohort of exceptional founders to join our 2026 ScaleUp programme and hope to see them on stage at the next Pitch Day," said Dave DeLucia, founder of Cascador. "Cascador's ScaleUp programme built upon my team's ability to translate learning into action by helping us refine our message and market position, adjust our funding strategy, and adapt without defensiveness. The Catalytic Fund due diligence team assessed Agriarche's financial strength, resourcefulness, and track record of success, and they rewarded our high-potential for scale and impact today by awarding a new NGN2.5 billion credit facility to power our growth," said Deina Mayaki of Agriarche, the largest funding recipient from the 2026 Catalytic Fund.

title: Stablecoins Are Not Killing the Naira. They Are Keeping Nigerian Businesses Alive
summary: The article argues that stablecoins like USDT and USDC are not undermining the Nigerian naira but are instead providing essential financial infrastructure for businesses and freelancers to manage currency volatility, preserve value, and facilitate international payments. It highlights Nigeria's $92.1 billion in crypto transactions in 2024, driven by utility, and mentions Monica.Cash as a platform bridging stablecoin-to-naira conversion.
content: The conversation around stablecoins in Nigeria often starts from the wrong premise, writes Mbah Casmir, founder and CEO of Monica Cash, a cryptocurrency-to-naira exchange platform helping individuals and businesses across Nigeria convert digital assets seamlessly. For years, digital assets, particularly dollar-backed stablecoins such as USDT and USDC, have been framed as threats to the naira. Critics argue that their growing adoption encourages capital flight, weakens confidence in the local currency, and accelerates dollarisation. While those concerns may sound reasonable, they overlook what is actually happening across Nigeria's economy. The reality is that most Nigerians are not turning to stablecoins because they want to speculate on cryptocurrency. They are turning to them because they are looking for practical ways to navigate economic uncertainty, preserve value, and keep businesses running. For thousands of small businesses, importers, exporters, freelancers, and remote workers, stablecoins have quietly become part of everyday financial planning. Consider the experience of a small importer sourcing products from suppliers abroad. A transaction that looks profitable on Monday can easily become a loss before payment is finally settled. For many businesses operating on tight margins, that kind of volatility makes it extremely difficult to plan with confidence. Every delay creates uncertainty, and every exchange rate movement can directly affect profitability. Stablecoins have become one of the tools helping businesses manage that risk. Rather than holding funds in a currency experiencing rapid fluctuations, many business owners temporarily preserve value in digital dollars until transactions are completed. This is not an attempt to replace the naira. It is a response to the realities of operating in a highly volatile financial environment. The same pattern is playing out among Nigerian freelancers and remote professionals. Nigeria has become one of Africa's leading hubs for digital talent, with professionals increasingly working for clients across North America, Europe, and other international markets. Yet receiving and managing international payments through traditional channels can still involve delays, friction, and conversion challenges. Stablecoins solves this problem by providing a faster and more flexible alternative that allows many professionals to exercise greater control over their earnings. This trend is not unique to Nigeria. Across emerging markets experiencing currency pressures, stablecoins are increasingly functioning as financial infrastructure. They are helping individuals and businesses preserve purchasing power, and participate more effectively in the global economy. What makes Nigeria particularly remarkable is the scale of adoption. According to recent blockchain analytics data, Nigerians processed approximately $92.1 billion in cryptocurrency transactions in 2024, making the country one of the most active crypto markets globally. A significant portion of that activity was driven by stablecoins, which tells an important story about how digital assets are being used in practice. Much of the demand is being driven by utility rather than speculation. The narrative that stablecoins are undermining the naira also overlooks a more important question: why are businesses turning to them in the first place? People generally adopt solutions that solve real problems. If stablecoin adoption continues to grow, it is because businesses and individuals are finding genuine value in the speed, accessibility, and financial flexibility these tools provide. In many cases, stablecoins are helping businesses protect working capital, manage risk more effectively, and maintain operational stability despite ongoing currency volatility. That growing reliance on stablecoins is also why companies like Monica.Cash have emerged, because the challenge for many users is no longer accessing stablecoins, but converting them quickly, securely, and efficiently whenever business needs arise. Monica.Cash was built to help bridge that gap by making stablecoin-to-naira transactions faster, simpler, and more accessible for individuals and businesses that increasingly rely on digital assets as part of their financial activities. Conversations within Monica.Cash, including ongoing discussions with COO, Chinazam Umezinwa, consistently reveal that much of the demand being seen today is no longer being driven by speculation.

title: Nigeria's StableKlin is a behavioural intelligence platform designed for cleaning businesses
summary: Nigerian startup StableKlin, founded by Dehinde Sogbanmu, offers a behavioural intelligence platform that helps cleaning businesses detect early signs of customer disengagement by analyzing booking patterns and behavioural changes. The self-funded startup is currently focused on the UK cleaning market and seeks pilot users to refine its dashboard and insights.
content: Nigerian startup StableKlin is a behavioural intelligence platform designed for cleaning businesses and other recurring service providers, which helps business owners understand something that is often difficult to see until it is too late - changes in customer behaviour. Most cleaning businesses already track bookings, customer details, invoices and payments. "What they often do not have is a clear way to identify when a loyal customer is gradually becoming less engaged. Customers rarely announce that they are leaving. More often, they start booking less frequently, delay appointments, become less predictable, or quietly drift away over time," said Dehinde Sogbanmu, StableKlin's founder. StableKlin is built to detect those early warning signs. The platform analyses patterns such as a customer's usual booking frequency, the time since their last service, changes in scheduling habits, consistency over time, and other behavioural indicators. It then highlights customers whose behaviour is beginning to move away from what has historically been normal for them. "For example, if a customer typically books every four weeks but has not booked for three months, that may indicate more than a simple delay. It could be an early sign that the relationship is weakening," said Sogbanmu. "The goal is to give business owners greater visibility into customer stability so they can take action earlier, strengthen retention, and reduce the risk of losing valuable repeat business." StableKlin was born from a real-world problem Sogbanmu encountered while running Potteries Prime Cleaning, a cleaning business in the UK. "As I worked with recurring customers, I noticed a pattern - customers rarely disappeared overnight. In most cases, there were subtle behavioural changes beforehand. Booking intervals became longer, routines became less consistent, and engagement gradually declined before revenue was affected," he said. "I realised that while there were plenty of tools available to help manage operations, there were very few tools focused on understanding customer behaviour itself." That insight led him to begin developing StableKlin. Rather than focusing on operational management, the startup focuses on behavioural insight. "It helps business owners identify changes in customer patterns before those changes become visible in revenue figures," said Sogbanmu. Currently self-funded, StableKlin has deliberately taken a lean approach to development, focusing on validating the core problem before investing heavily in additional features or infrastructure. "One of the most significant milestones so far has been transforming the concept from an observation made while running a cleaning business into a working product with a functional dashboard," said Sogbanmu. "The platform can already analyse customer booking behaviour, identify deviations from normal patterns and surface behavioural insights that may indicate customer drift. Another important milestone has been beginning conversations with business owners and sharing the broader vision publicly. This includes publishing content around behavioural intelligence in service businesses, engaging with founders and operators, and introducing the concept to industry and technology audiences." At this stage, the focus remains on learning, refining the product and ensuring that the insights generated are genuinely useful to the businesses it is designed to serve. "The platform has a working dashboard and behavioural analysis capabilities, and I am actively seeking cleaning-business owners and service operators who are willing to test the concept and provide feedback," said Sogbanmu. "Although it is still early, the response to the idea itself has been encouraging. Many business owners immediately recognise the problem because they have experienced it firsthand. They understand that customers often do not leave suddenly; instead, their behaviour changes gradually before they eventually stop booking." StableKlin is currently focused on the UK cleaning industry. "I chose this market because it is where I have direct operational experience and a deep understanding of the challenges faced by business owners. Cleaning businesses rely heavily on trust, consistency and recurring customer relationships, making them an ideal starting point for behavioural analysis," said Sogbanmu. "Looking ahead, I believe the same principles can be applied across a much wider range of recurring service businesses."

title: How Zimbabwe's Maminda Agri-Fintech helps smallholders access finance, advisory, markets
summary: Zimbabwean startup Maminda Agri-Fintech provides smallholder farmers with an integrated digital platform combining AI-powered farming support, digital financial services, satellite crop monitoring, cooperative financing, and market access tools. The bootstrapped startup is in pilot stage with fewer than 100 active users in Mashonaland and Manicaland, preparing for a pre-seed round and planning expansion to Zambia and Malawi.
content: Zimbabwean startup Maminda Agri-Fintech is helping smallholder farmers access finance, agricultural advisory services and markets through a single digital ecosystem. Founded last year, Maminda Agri-Fintech combines AI-powered farming support, digital financial services, satellite crop monitoring, cooperative financing and market access tools designed specifically for underserved farming communities. Farmers can access personalised crop recommendations, monitor crop health using satellite data, participate in digital savings and financing groups through its Smart Digital Mukando system, build alternative credit profiles, and connect directly with buyers and input suppliers. "Maminda operates across the entire agricultural value chain, supporting farmers from season planning and input financing through production, monitoring and post-harvest marketing," Maminda Edward Gandanzara, the startup's founder and CEO, told Disrupt Africa. Smallholder farmers produce a significant proportion of Africa's food, but remain among the most financially excluded groups. Many lack formal credit histories, limiting access to financing, quality inputs and profitable markets. "Most existing solutions address only one challenge at a time, whether agronomic advisory, agricultural financing or market access. Maminda's approach is to integrate these services into a single platform designed around the realities of smallholder farming," said Gandanzara. Competitors include agricultural financing and advisory platforms operating across Africa, such as Apollo Agriculture and other regional agri-fintech providers. In Zimbabwe, however, there are currently few platforms combining cooperative financing, AI-driven credit profiling, crop monitoring and marketplace services within a single ecosystem. The bootstrapped Maminda, which is preparing for a pre-seed fundraising round to support product development, pilot expansion and market growth, has won a host of awards already. Yet it is still at the pilot stage, working with a small group of farmers in Zimbabwe to validate product-market fit and refine the platform's financial and agronomic models. "The company currently has fewer than 100 active users. At this stage, the focus is on learning, validation and measurable farmer outcomes rather than rapid user acquisition," said Gandanzara. Maminda currently operates in Zimbabwe, with pilot activities concentrated in farming communities in Mashonaland and Manicaland. "The company's medium-term expansion strategy targets neighbouring agricultural markets including Zambia and Malawi, before broader expansion into Southern and Eastern Africa," said Gandanzara. Maminda's planned revenue streams include transaction fees on marketplace activities, financing-related income, software subscriptions for agricultural organisations and data analytics services for agribusinesses and financial institutions. The company is currently pre-revenue as it focuses on product validation and pilot deployment.

title: Les résultats du Baromètre EY 2026 des entreprises en Tunisie
summary: EY publie les résultats de son baromètre 2026 sur les entreprises tunisiennes, offrant une analyse des tendances, défis et perspectives du tissu économique national.
content: L'article présente les principaux enseignements du Baromètre EY 2026 pour la Tunisie, incluant la performance des entreprises, les attentes des dirigeants et les axes de développement prioritaires. Le rapport couvre les secteurs clés, l'impact du contexte macroéconomique et les recommandations pour renforcer la compétitivité.

title: Pan-African WRAPPED helping people find jobs in film industry
summary: WRAPPED, a pan-African platform founded by Zoe Ramushu, centralizes job hiring in the film and television industry, replacing fragmented WhatsApp groups and spreadsheets. The startup gained 600 sign-ups in its first days and secured a partnership with MultiChoice.
content: "WRAPPED is the app that I wish I had when I was trying to break into this industry. Right now, hiring in film and television is done through WhatsApp groups, Facebook groups, spreadsheets, and institutional memory captured in human brains," WRAPPED founder Zoe Ramushu tells Disrupt Africa. "A message drops in a WhatsApp group, a gig is gone in two minutes. You didn't even know what that job was. Two minutes." Ramushu has come up with a solution. What WRAPPED does is give the industry one centralised place where everybody is, where all the jobs are getting booked out of, at the click of a button. "We're categorised by skills and experience level so you find exactly who you're looking for. We cut down 33 per cent of the pre-production workload. It sounds simple and it is, that's the innovation," Ramushu said. She said the idea for WRAPPED came up based on her own experiences. "I co-founded Swift (Sisters Working in Film and Television) and spent five-to-six years voluntarily building that organisation because I believe in being part of the change I'd like to see. Then I got my first Netflix project. Close to 120 people on set. I said to my line producer, we need at least one female in every department. Silence in the room. Like I was joking. We found them, a female in every single department. But it was an exercise," said Ramushu. "Time-consuming. In production, time is literally money. That was the moment I said this cannot be the way things work. WRAPPED was real and existed in my mind from that moment. And glad today it's now real for everyone else." The startup had 600 sign-ups in the first two to three days, with minimal marketing. "That just spoke to the desire, the complete demand, for people working in this industry to have a centralised place to gather. A place that allows them to connect and be seen," said Ramushu. "Our partnership with MultiChoice has been invaluable. They've been championing African stories for decades and having meaningful funding as well as their support has really just changed the shape of what we're building." Ramushu said she doesn't see WRAPPED as an African platform. "It is built by Africans but it is a global platform. If it's good tech, it's good tech. If it's a good product, it's a good product. Same as film. Our presence at Berlin, at Cannes, three years running, those invitations are evidence that we are global. That's not a plan. That's already happening," she said. The platform is free for freelancers and agents, production companies can choose a subscription that best suits their needs.

title: Flat6Labs, IFC launch StartAlgeria capacity-building programme
summary: Egypt's Flat6Labs, the International Finance Corporation (IFC), and Algeria's Ministry of Knowledge Economy, Startups and Micro-Enterprises have launched StartAlgeria, a capacity-building programme targeting entrepreneur support organisations (ESOs) in Algeria. The programme aims to strengthen ESOs' ability to support pre-seed and seed-stage startups through workshops, masterclasses, and six months of post-programme mentorship, focusing on startup selection, program design, investment readiness, fundraising strategy, and financial sustainability. The pilot cohort will focus on incubators in Algiers.
content: Egypt's Flat6Labs, the IFC and Algeria's Ministry of Knowledge Economy, Startups and Micro-Enterprises have launched StartAlgeria, a capacity-building programme that puts entrepreneur support organisations (ESOs) at the forefront of Algeria's ecosystem future. StartAlgeria comes at a key moment for Algeria's entrepreneurship landscape, shifting the focus toward improving how the ESOs operate by providing them with international best practices adapted to each organization's needs, a community-driven approach that focuses on peer learning, and facilitating connections with investors, policymakers, and key stakeholders. The programme is designed to equip Algerian ESOs reinforcing pre-seed and seed-stage startups with the expertise, frameworks, and networks needed to contribute to a stronger, more competitive entrepreneurship ecosystem in Algeria and expand into global markets. StartAlgeria will pilot a first cohort focusing on incubators in the capital, Algiers. Following a call for application, the selected ESOs will go through a structured programme comprising workshops and masterclasses covering key areas such as startup selection, program design and delivery, and investment readiness. In addition to the core programme, participating ESOs will benefit from six months of post-programme mentorship, focusing on areas such as fundraising strategy, partnership development, financial sustainability, and programme improvement. This sustained engagement's goal is to provide a lasting impact in how Algerian ESOs operate and what they're able to offer the startups they champion. "Algeria's startup ecosystem is demonstrating remarkable potential and a rapidly growing level of maturity, driven by an ambitious new generation of founders, increasing institutional support, and a strong national commitment to innovation and entrepreneurship," said Yehia Houry, CEO of Flat6Labs. "The opportunity today lies in further empowering entrepreneurship support organisations to match this momentum by strengthening their ability to identify and nurture high-potential startups, deliver impactful and results-driven programs, and create stronger connections between entrepreneurs and sources of capital. With the right support structures in place, Algeria is well positioned to become one of the leading innovation hubs in the region."

title: Katapult Africa opens applications for latest climate-focused accelerator programme
summary: Katapult Africa has opened applications for its three-month climate-tech accelerator programme, offering equity investment of $150,000-$500,000, workshops, mentorship, and investor access to early-stage African startups in agritech, climate-smart agriculture, and resilient food systems.
content: Applications are open for the Katapult Africa accelerator programme, which is designed to support climate-tech startups in reaching key milestones - growth, investor readiness, and impact management. The three-month Katapult Africa programme gives participants access to intensive workshops, learning sessions, and a broad network of key stakeholders, including investors and industry experts. This equips them with the tools, knowledge, and connections needed to scale their impact across the continent. Participating ventures will also receive between US$150,000 and US$500,000 in equity investment. Katapult Africa is looking for impact-driven early-stage climate-tech startups operating in Africa, led by experienced teams, with strong paths to commercialisation or early revenues, and scalable business models. The focus is pan-African, across the following impact areas - agritech, climate-smart agriculture, and resilient food systems.

title: African tech startups raise $260m in Q2 2026; down 40% on same period last year
summary: Thirty-eight African tech startups raised $260 million in Q2 2026, a 40% decline from $427 million in Q2 2025. The annual African Tech Startups Funding Report by Disrupt Africa indicates a potential funding dip for 2026, with projected annual total of $1.28 billion, down from $1.64 billion in 2025.
content: Thirty-eight African tech startups raised a total of US$260 million in the second quarter of 2026, down 40 per cent on the US$427 million raised in the same period of 2025. Total investment into the African tech startup ecosystem increased by almost 50 per cent to US$1.64 billion in 2025 as the sector began to slowly recover from the impacts of the global capital shortage, according to the 11th edition of the annual African Tech Startups Funding Report released by startup news and research portal Disrupt Africa. Numbers from Q1 2026 had suggested this year would at least match, and probably surpass, 2025, with 40 startups banking US$382,150,000 in funding. Yet in Q2, 38 startups raised just $260 million, substantially down from the same period last year, and raising concerns African tech could be in for another funding dip. Extrapolated over the course of the year, that would be 156 startups (down from 178 in 2025), and an annual 2026 total of US$1.28 billion, down from US$1.6 billion in 2025.

title: Tunisie | 2 000 voitures électriques seront vendues d'ici fin 2026
summary: Le marché tunisien des véhicules électriques a connu une forte accélération au premier quadrimestre 2026 avec plus de 1 100 unités vendues, contre 600 sur l'ensemble de 2025. La filière vise 2 000 ventes d'ici fin 2026, portée par de nouvelles marques, un cadre incitatif (allégements fiscaux, réduction de TVA, aides à l'achat) et un intérêt croissant des consommateurs. Les défis restent le coût d'achat, le réseau de bornes de recharge et la maintenance spécialisée.
content: Le marché des voitures électriques a connu en Tunisie une forte accélération au cours des quatre premiers mois de 2026. De janvier à avril, plus de 1 100 véhicules électriques ont été vendus, contre moins de 600 pour l'ensemble de l'année 2025. Selon Ibrahim Debache, président de la Chambre syndicale nationale des concessionnaires et des constructeurs automobiles, qui parlait à l'agence Tap, en marge de la 4e édition de l'Industry Innovation Day, organisé le jeudi 4 juin 2026, au Sheraton Tunis, par la Chambre de commerce et d'industrie tuniso-allemande (AHK Tunisie), en collaboration avec l'Agence allemande de coopération internationale (GIZ), et la Tunisian Automotive Association (TAA), sous le thème «Ecosystème tunisien : Servir l'industrie automobile de demain», ces données confirment le début d'une nouvelle phase pour un segment encore émergent mais en pleine croissance, porté par l'arrivée de nouvelles marques, l'élargissement de l'offre commerciale et un intérêt croissant des consommateurs pour des solutions de mobilité perçues comme plus modernes et moins polluantes. L'événement était consacré aux transformations de l'industrie automobile, notamment la nouvelle mobilité, l'innovation technologique, la numérisation, le développement durable et les nouvelles compétences requises. Le secteur vise désormais la vente d'environ 2 000 voitures électriques d'ici fin 2026, un objectif jugé réaliste par les opérateurs si le rythme de croissance observé en début d'année se maintient. Le nouveau cadre incitatif tunisien, destiné à promouvoir la mobilité électrique et hybride rechargeable, contribue également à soutenir la demande, grâce à des allégements fiscaux, des réductions de TVA et des aides à l'achat pour certaines catégories professionnelles et institutionnelles. Malgré cette croissance, le secteur reste encore à ses débuts. Les voitures électriques ne représentent toujours qu'une part limitée du marché automobile tunisien, où le coût d'achat, le pouvoir d'achat des ménages et la disponibilité des services après-vente continuent d'influencer les décisions des consommateurs. Le principal défi demeure le développement des infrastructures de recharge. Les opérateurs soulignent également la nécessité de maîtriser les prix, de renforcer la maintenance spécialisée et d'assurer une meilleure couverture des bornes de recharge, conditions jugées essentielles pour pérenniser la croissance actuelle. Pour la Tunisie, la mobilité électrique s'inscrit dans une stratégie plus large de transition énergétique et de réduction de la dépendance aux énergies fossiles. Les autorités visent à développer progressivement le parc de véhicules électriques et le réseau de recharge d'ici 2030, mais la transition d'un marché de niche à un segment central dépendra de la capacité à combiner incitations, infrastructures et accessibilité financière. I. B.

title: Beltone, Telda partner to expand digital investing access in Egypt
summary: Beltone Asset Management, a subsidiary of Beltone Holding, has partnered with Egyptian fintech Telda to offer Beltone's investment products and mutual funds through Telda's digital platform. The collaboration provides Telda users with zero-commission access to funds such as Meya Meya, Sabayek, B-Secure, and Wafra EGX 33, aiming to enhance financial inclusion in Egypt.
content: Beltone Asset Management, a subsidiary of Beltone Holding, has partnered with Egyptian fintech company Telda, which specialises in digital financial services, to broaden access to Beltone's investment products and mutual funds through Telda's digital platform. The partnership aims to simplify access to investment solutions by enabling Telda users to invest in funds established and managed by Beltone Asset Management through a seamless digital experience that offers zero commission fees, excluding precious metals funds, with the ability to transfer redeemed proceeds directly to the Telda card, supporting broader financial inclusion and expanding participation in investment products across Egypt. By combining Beltone Asset Management's institutional expertise and established track record in asset management with Telda's user-centric digital infrastructure, the collaboration delivers streamlined access to a diversified suite of investment solutions through a fully integrated platform. Under the partnership, Telda users will gain access to a broad range of funds established and managed by Beltone Asset Management, including the "Meya Meya" fund, the "Sabayek" gold investment fund, "B-Secure," which offers a high level of liquidity, as well as "Wafra EGX 33," the Shariah-compliant equity fund, alongside a selection of sector-focused investment funds. "This partnership marks an important step toward delivering a more integrated financial experience for our users by bringing together everyday financial services and investment solutions within a single platform, enabling our users to access trusted investment products through a simple and flexible digital experience," said Ahmed Sabbah, CEO of Telda.

title: SA's Supascale launches Africa's first GPU cloud marketplace
summary: South African startup Supascale has launched Africa's first GPU cloud marketplace, making advanced AI models more affordable while allowing GPU owners to earn passive income.
content: South African startup Supascale has launched Africa's first GPU cloud marketplace, making advanced AI models more affordable while allowing GPU owners to earn passive income. Founded by Aaron Bornmann, Supascale enables users to access leading AI models at significantly lower costs, while having the world's fastest GPU onboarding process for GPU owners to monetise unused computing power through a cloud AI marketplace. As demand for AI continues to surge globally, and AI data centres are putting increasing strain on the environment through excessive noise, electricity and water resources usage for cooling, Supascale offers a green-alternative to accessing AI models powered by idle GPUs from laptops, PCs and unused GPUs in data centres - allowing everyday people to earn from the AI boom. "The AI economy needs to be accessible to everyone, not just large hyperscalers," said Bornmann. "We're building the infrastructure that allows businesses, developers, creators, and innovators to access world-class AI capabilities while enabling everyday GPU owners to participate directly in the AI economy." The company plans to expand its footprint internationally while positioning Africa as an active participant in the rapidly growing global AI infrastructure market.

title: Nigeria's MyAgent launches to build trust level for African real estate
summary: Nigerian prop-tech startup MyAgent has launched a platform connecting renters directly with verified landlords, aiming to build trust and transparency in Africa's real estate market, starting with Nigeria's rental sector. Founded by Samuel Afolabi, the startup is currently founder-funded, generating revenue through a 20% commission on rental transactions, and is now fundraising to accelerate supply-side growth.
content: Nigerian startup MyAgent has launched a prop-tech platform it says is building the trust and transaction layer for real estate in Africa, starting with Nigeria's rental market. Launched earlier this year by Samuel Afolabi, MyAgent connects renters directly with verified landlords, reduces dependence on agents, and makes the rental process faster, more transparent, and less expensive. "The long-term vision is to become the default infrastructure for property transactions, covering rentals first, then land, sales, shortlets, and eventually the data layer for the market," Afolabi told Disrupt Africa. Most real estate platforms in Nigeria focus on listings, but the real problem is trust and supply. "Renters want better access, but landlords are the hard side of the marketplace to win. Most platforms underestimate how important it is to actively acquire and verify supply, not just attract demand," Afolabi said. Though MyAgent's competition includes traditional agents, listing platforms, and rental marketplaces, it believes the winning model is the one that solves landlord acquisition, verification, and trust first. Currently founder-funded, the startup has already launched its product, onboarded landlords and renters, completed transactions, and is generating initial revenue. "We are now fundraising to accelerate growth, especially on the supply side," Afolabi said. MyAgent's immediate focus is building density and trust locally, before expanding. "Long-term, we plan to grow into property sales, land, and shortlets, and eventually expand into other African markets where the same problem exists," he said. The startup makes money through a 20 per cent commission on successful rental transactions.

title: SOS Racisme solidaire avec Saâdia Mosbah
summary: L'organisation SOS Racisme dénonce la condamnation de la militante antiraciste Saâdia Mosbah à 8 ans de prison pour délits financiers, qu'elle considère comme une répression politique ciblée contre les défenseurs des droits des minorités et un recul des libertés démocratiques en Tunisie.
content: La Cour d'appel de Tunis a confirmé ce mardi 23 juin la lourde condamnation de la militante antiraciste Saâdia Mosbah à 8 ans de prison pour délits financiers. Pour l'organisation SOS Racisme, qui soutient la présidente de l'association Mnemty, ces accusations masquent « une répression politique ciblée contre les défenseurs des droits des minorités et un recul alarmant des libertés démocratiques acquises depuis 2011 ». Derrière ces motifs de façade, la vraie raison de la persécution judiciaire vécue par Saâdia et plusieurs membres de son association « M'nemty » (« Mon rêve »), c'est le racisme antinoir. Car Saâdia est noire et, avec les militants de l'association qu'elle a cofondée et qu'elle présidait, elle défendait les droits des personnes noires en Tunisie. Son activisme a contribué à l'adoption d'une loi pionnière dans le monde arabe : la loi du 23 octobre 2018 tendant à lutter contre les discriminations. Mais, à partir de 2023, le pouvoir tunisien incarné par l'autocrate Kaïs Saïed a décidé de reprendre à son compte les délires relatifs à un prétendu « grand remplacement » des populations arabo-berbères par des populations subsahariennes. C'est dans ce contexte que Saâdia a été arrêtée et emprisonnée en mai 2024 pour ne plus jamais recouvrer la liberté. SOS Racisme dénonce le racisme antinoir dont Saâdia Mosbah est victime et se tient aux côtés de celles et de ceux qui, en Tunisie, subissent la répression d'un pouvoir qui promeut le racisme et tente par tous les moyens de détruire la société civile et plus généralement les acquis démocratiques de la révolution de 2011.

title: SA's stub is an AI-native accounting platform built for everyday entrepreneurs
summary: South African startup stub has built an AI-native accounting platform designed for micro-entrepreneurs and small businesses, offering simple tools for invoicing, quotes, payments, and cashflow management with a full double-entry ledger. Founded in 2023, stub has partnered with iKhokha, Capitec, and Yoco to distribute its platform, now supporting nearly 10,000 businesses.
content:
South African startup stub has built an AI-native accounting platform "built for everyday entrepreneurs", doing all the things a small business needs, wrapped in something simple and intelligent. Founded in 2023, stub offers easy-to-use tools for sending invoices, managing quotes, tracking payments, and monitoring cashflow. Underneath that simplicity is a full double-entry ledger and customisable chart of accounts, so the books are real and compliant. Increasingly, the platform uses automation and intelligent data models to categorise transactions, reconcile against linked bank feeds and surface insights - automating the manual work so the business owner spends time on their business rather than on paperwork and everyday admin.
The founding insight was personal, said Tayla Dandridge, co-founder and CEO of stub. "Having spent most of my career in financial services focused on small and micro businesses, I kept seeing the same thing - accounting tools were too complicated, too expensive, and too far removed from how people actually run a business day to day," she told Disrupt Africa. "Traditional accounting platforms are generally built for accountants and established SMEs. They are powerful, but often too complex, time-consuming and costly for a tuck shop, a salon, freelancer or a side hustle."
The real "competition" for most of stub's market isn't another platform at all, Dandridge said. "It's a spreadsheet, a WhatsApp chat, or a shoebox full of slips. That is the gap we spotted at the bottom of the pyramid: millions of micro-entrepreneurs and small business owners who were completely unserved by traditional accounting software," she said.
The startup, which bootstrapped a large portion of its journey until it found strategic angel investors, has secured a few major partnerships, which Dandridge says matter most. "iKhokha was our first big partnership - stub powers their iK Accounting product, and that single anchor relationship has driven a significant number of businesses onto our platform," she said. "The second was our direct integration with Capitec, South Africa's largest bank by customer numbers, which puts simple accounting in the hands of a huge base of entrepreneurs and side-hustlers. And most recently, our direct app integration with Yoco, the payment platform for independent business owners across the country."
Going from a single anchor partner, to a direct bank integration, to now working with Yoco in roughly 18 months are milestones Dandridge said she is proud of. "But the most exciting part is the access this creates for entrepreneurs who have never had this level of tooling to run their businesses," she said.
As a result, access has been strong and accelerating. "We're now supporting just under 10,000 businesses on the stub platform, up from under 4,000 entrepreneurs roughly seven months ago. Signups have compounded every quarter - from a few hundred in 2023 to over 1,800 in a single quarter this year," said Dandridge.
stub's strongest concentrations are Cape Town, Johannesburg, Pretoria and Durban, and its customer base skews toward micro and informal businesses like tuck shops, spazas, taverns, fast food outlets, salons, cafés, independent consultants and service-based entrepreneurs.
"Our name is a statement of intent: stub is built for entrepreneurs. Platforms make global expansion a simpler task, and the playbook stays the same - simple tools distributed through partners and our own direct-to-consumer, on-the-ground channels," Dandridge said.
The startup runs off a subscription model. Businesses use the core tools for free and upgrade to paid plans for the fuller feature set, with payments revenue layered on through payment links.
Dandridge said stub has had to overcome plenty of challenges, but has learnt that difficulties usually carry an opportunity inside them. "An unhappy customer is often one of the best teachers; almost every time, they are pointing at something that makes the product better for everyone else. And there's also a handful of hard "no"s along the way - and more often than not, that pushes us toward something exciting just around the corner," she said. "So, we try not to treat the hard moments as setbacks. They've shaped the product, the team, and how we show up for entrepreneurs - who, after all, deal with this kind of stuff every single day."

title: LemFi Acquires Wealth8 to Expand into Wealth Management and Investment Services
summary: LemFi, a cross-border financial platform, has received UK FCA approval to acquire investment platform Wealth8, enabling it to offer wealth-building and investment services to its 2 million+ customers. The acquisition marks LemFi's entry into wealthtech and its evolution into a full financial-life platform for diaspora communities.
content: LemFi, the financial platform for people living and working across borders, has secured approval from the UK's Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) for its acquisition of investment platform Wealth8. The approval paves the way for LemFi to add wealth-building and investment to its product ecosystem, marking the company's entry into wealthtech and a significant step in its evolution into a full financial-life platform. LemFi is the trusted platform helping people move money across borders, access credit, and save money across the UK, Europe, North America and Australia. The Wealth8 acquisition adds a missing piece: the ability to grow money over the long term. It continues an arc familiar to millions of people: build life in a new country, support family back home, save, establish a credit footprint, and ultimately invest in a more secure future. With Wealth8, LemFi can now support that journey on a single platform. That momentum is already underway. In 2025, LemFi launched its Instant Access Savings Account, powered by ClearBank: a high-yield product that lets customers earn daily interest, paid monthly, on balances they can access at any time. With promotional rates reaching 5.00% AER, it was a deliberate move to shift from just enabling customers to move money to helping customers keep and grow it. But Savings is only the first step in building lasting wealth; investing is also key, and it is precisely this crucial part of the financial ladder that newcomers are most excluded from. The opportunity is significant. While investing remains one of the UK's most powerful drivers of long-term wealth, participation remains uneven. Most UK adults with more than £10,000 in investible assets (61%) hold at least three-quarters of it in cash rather than investments. The barriers are sharpest for communities that have arrived from abroad. Research from the London School of Economics published in late 2025 found that the UK's ethnic wealth gap has widened over the past decade. Runnymede Trust research revealed that for every £1 of wealth, several minority communities hold as little as 10-20p. The gap reflects unequal income, but also access to the assets that compound into wealth over time. Wealth8 was founded to address exactly this divide, with a mission to make investing simple, affordable, and accessible to communities that mainstream platforms have overlooked, offering minimums as low as £8 and diversified portfolios. Under LemFi, that mission will broaden to reach the wider community of people who live and work across borders, combining Wealth8's investment expertise with LemFi's scale, technology and 2 million+ customer base. The FCA's approval reflects LemFi's growing regulatory footprint and governance capabilities, building on existing authorisations in the UK and approvals across North America, Europe, Australia and key remittance corridors in Africa and Asia. Ridwan Olalere, co-founder and CEO of LemFi, said: "We started LemFi by helping people send money because that was the most urgent need. But financial progress doesn't stop at the transfer. This approval allows us to help customers save, access credit and now invest, supporting them as they build long-term financial security wherever they call home." This acquisition is the latest milestone in LemFi's expansion from a remittance specialist into a multi-product financial platform, following its move into credit, connectivity and savings, as well as a series of market and regulatory approvals over the past year. The company has committed to deepening its presence in the UK, which it sees as a leading global hub for cross-border finance. About LemFi Founded in 2021, LemFi is building the future of financial services for the underserved worldwide. Initially focused on remittances, with over two million customers across Europe, Australia, and North America sending funds to more than 30 emerging markets, the company is expanding into a full-service financial hub for the communities it serves, spanning payments, credit, connectivity, savings, and now investing. LemFi's team consists of over 350 people across four continents, with more than $85 million in funding from investors including Highland Europe.
