Benbouhali, cette réunion reflète la volonté du gouvernement de stimuler les souscriptions aux titres publics dans un contexte marqué par une marge de manœuvre financière limitée et une hausse des dépenses publiques. Toutefois, l'expert a souligné que cette dynamique place les banques face à un exercice d'équilibriste délicat : répondre aux besoins de financement de l'État sans comprimer le crédit destiné au secteur privé - un crédit essentiel pour l'investissement, la production et l'emploi. Effet d'éviction aux dépens du secteur privé L'analyste attribue la pression sur les finances publiques tunisiennes à des facteurs structurels : un déficit budgétaire persistant, le coût des subventions à l'énergie et aux produits de première nécessité, la faiblesse des recettes fiscales et le poids de l'économie informelle, qui représenterait environ 40 % de l'activité économique. Cette situation priverait l'État d'environ 12 milliards de dinars de recettes fiscales annuelles. La tendance croissante des banques à orienter leurs liquidités vers l'achat de bons du Trésor peut donc engendrer un «effet d'éviction» , réduisant ainsi leur capacité à financer les entreprises. Cette question est d'autant plus sensible que le budget 2026 prévoit des dépenses d'environ 63,6 milliards de dinars (19,5 milliards d'euros), des recettes de 52,6 milliards (15,93 milliards d'euros) et un déficit dépassant légèrement les 11 milliards (3,33 milliards d'euros). Les besoins de financement globaux demeurent élevés et, selon des estimations citées par des sources financières internationales, s'élèvent à environ 27 milliards de dinars (8,18 milliards d'euros). Cette question est également au cœur des analyses de la Banque mondiale, qui a récemment souligné la dépendance croissante de la Tunisie à l'égard du financement intérieur, la part de ce dernier atteignant 77 % de la dette publique en 2024. Les observateurs notent que l'enjeu consiste à éviter que le recours au système bancaire national n'entrave la reprise de l'investissement privé, déjà limité par une croissance modérée, des contraintes réglementaires et l'incertitude macroéconomique. Pour atténuer ce risque, Benbouhali préconise des réformes structurelles des finances publiques : rationalisation des dépenses, refonte progressive du système de subventions, élargissement de l'assiette fiscale et intégration graduelle de l'économie informelle dans le secteur formel. Selon l'expert, de telles mesures permettraient de réduire la dépendance de l'État à l'emprunt intérieur et de libérer des ressources bancaires au profit du secteur productif.

title: FinTech app built to help students manage finances raises $40M Series B
summary: San Francisco-based fintech startup Mos, founded by Tunisian entrepreneur Amira Yahyaoui, raised a $40 million Series B round led by Tiger Global, valuing the company at $400 million. Other investors include Sequoia, Khaled Helioui, Expa, Emerson Collective, and Lux Capital.
content: San Francisco-based Mos just announced a $40 million Series B to grow its platform that provides financial services to students. The round valued the startup at $400 million and was led by Tiger Global. Other investors included Sequoia, Khaled Helioui, Expa, Emerson Collective and Lux Capital, according to the announcement. It was previously valued at $48 million after its Series A in early 2020, according to PitchBook. Mos was founded in 2017 by Amira Yahyaoui, 37, who named the company after the fictional town of Mos Espa from Star Wars, according to its website. She's also from Tataouine, Tunisia - the real life inspiration for Luke Skywalker's fictional home.

title: Wattnow raises $1.3 million in pre-series A funding
summary: Tunisian startup Wattnow closed a $1.3 million pre-Series A round led by Katapult, 216 Capital Ventures, Launch Africa Ventures, and a follow-on from Oman Technology Fund. CrossBoundary and an angel investor also participated. The round was preceded by a bridge from Bridging Angels and support from Startup Tunisia via the Air2 program.
content: We are pleased to announce that Wattnow has just closed a $1.3 million pre-series A round led by Katapult, 216 Capital Ventures, Launch Africa Ventures and a follow-on from Oman Technology Fund. CrossBoundary and an angel investor also participated. This round was preceded by a very important bridge from Bridging Angels and the support of Startup Tunisia in the form of the Air2 program.

title: Iran | Unité de façade pour les adieux à Khamenei !
summary: L'article couvre les funérailles de l'ayatollah Ali Khamenei en Iran, les tensions politiques internes, et une série de brèves actualités tunisiennes (coupures d'eau, agression d'un médecin, don d'Ons Jabeur, événement culturel à Mahdia, etc.).
content: Les funérailles de l'ayatollah Ali Khamenei et des membres de sa famille assassinés le 28 février 2026 ont débuté le 3 juillet et se poursuivront jusqu'au 9 juillet à Mashhad. Cette semaine funéraire offre un répit dans les tensions autour des négociations avec les États-Unis. Le New York Times décrit une mise en scène d'unité entre rivaux politiques iraniens. Parallèlement, des voix radicales réclament la vengeance plutôt que la négociation. La participation du fils Mojtaba Khamenei reste incertaine. En Tunisie, on note : le CRLDHT demande la libération de détenus, des coupures d'eau à Ben Arous et Zaghouan, l'agression d'un médecin à Monastir, un don d'équipement médical par Ons Jabeur, un plan d'urgence demandé par le Cnom, la création de réserves marines, et la Semaine de la mer à Mahdia.

title: How Akuna Wallet, Trenderz want to fix creator payments in Africa
summary: Africa's creator economy faces significant payment infrastructure gaps, excluding millions of Francophone African creators from platform monetization. Startups like Akuna Wallet, Trenderz, and Mainstack are building payment infrastructure to enable cross-border payouts, standardized contracts, and reliable payment systems for creators in countries like Côte d'Ivoire, Cameroon, Benin, and DRC.
content: Africa's creator economy has a payment problem. These startups want to fix it. By Lina Kacyem, 2nd Jun, 2026. Africa hosts millions of digital content creators, a booming social media landscape, and one of the planet's youngest populations. Yet financial infrastructure to pay them fairly is virtually non-existent. The creator economy on the continent is expanding rapidly. According to the International School of Advertising, the creator economy market was valued between $3 and $5.1 billion in 2025 and is projected to reach $29.84 billion by 2032, with a CAGR of 28.7%. Smartphones are proliferating rapidly. TikTok, Instagram, and YouTube audiences are growing faster in Africa than anywhere else. Yet for the vast majority of African creators - particularly those in Francophone West Africa - one question remains unanswered: how to get paid? The most visible expression of this gap is the exclusion of most African countries from direct monetization programs on platforms where creators build their livelihoods. In 2026, the YouTube Partner Program is available in over 120 countries, but eligibility is not universal - creators must reside in a supported region to access monetization. For creators in Côte d'Ivoire, Cameroon, Benin, or the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC) - countries with tens of millions of social media users - YouTube ad revenue remains structurally inaccessible through conventional channels. Reasons accumulate: global platforms offering monetization often require an international bank account or do not accept local banks for payouts. PayPal coverage is partial or non-existent. Stripe remains unavailable in most Francophone African markets. A creator with 500,000 subscribers in Abidjan, building audiences for international brands, still cannot receive a YouTube payout directly to a local bank account. Although Africa represents 18% of the world's population, the continent captures only about 5.4% of global content creation revenue - a gap explained not by production volume or audience engagement, but by payment infrastructure failures. The problem extends beyond platform payouts. Influencer marketing in the region operates through a fragmented network of WhatsApp negotiations, cash payments, in-kind arrangements - dinners and hotel stays as fees - and informal one-off transfers that can take weeks to execute. There are no standardized contracts, no performance tracking infrastructure, no reliable cross-border payment system. The result: an economically significant market still operating artisanally. Building the pipes: Akuna, Mainstack and the new infrastructure players. A new generation of platforms is attempting to build this missing infrastructure, attacking the problem from different angles.

title: Startup Tunisia: The Fund of Funds' launch event
summary: The official launch of the projects "Startups and Innovative SMEs" and "Flywheel", two funding mechanisms and support for startups, marked a major turning point for Tunisia's innovation ecosystem. The ceremony took place at Ennejma Ezzahra Palace in Sidi Bou Said, attended by the Minister of Technology and Communication Fadhel Kraiem, German Ambassador Peter Prügel, World Bank head of operations Tony Verheijen, Smart Capital Chairman Samir Saied, and CDC Director General Boutheina Ben Yaghlane.
content: Tuesday, March 23 will forever mark a major turning point for the innovation ecosystem in Tunisia. It was the day of the official launch of the projects "Startups and Innovative SMEs" and "Flywheel", two funding mechanisms and support for startups and beating heart of the program Startup Tunisia. The launch ceremony took place at the Ennejma Ezzahra Palace in Sidi Bou Said, in the presence of the Minister of Technology and Communication, Fadhel Kraiem, His Excellency the German Ambassador to Tunisia Peter Prügel, the head of operations for Tunisia at the World Bank, Tony Verheijen, the Chairman of the Board of Directors of Smart Capital, Samir Saied, and the Director General of the Caisse des Dépôts et Consignations (CDC), Boutheina Ben Yaghlane.

title: AVIDEA completes a round of financing of 2 million dinars
summary: Tunisian insurtech startup AVIDEA raised 2 million dinars in a Pre-Series A round led by Capsa Capital Partners and 216 CAPITAL FUND I to scale its digital claims and fraud detection products.
content: The Tunisian startup AVIDEA announced to have closed a Pre-Series A round of financing of 2 million dinars led by one of the main investors in Private Equity in Tunisia, Capsa Capital Partners, and the specialized investment fund 216 CAPITAL FUND I managed by the management company 216 CAPITAL VENTURES. The products currently offered by AVIDEA namely DigiClaim and DigiFraud, respectively for the digitization of the automobile claim and the fight against fraud, being already validated and in production at its customers, the Startup is now moving towards a decisive phase of industrialization and internalization. Created in 2017, AVIDEA is a startup specialized in the digitalization of the automobile claim and the fight against fraud. Its main mission is to help insurance companies reduce the cost of the car claim while offering a better user experience to their customers.

title: Spotify Bets On AI Podcasts, Smarter Ads, And Fan Experiences At 2026 Investor Day
summary: At its 2026 Investor Day, Spotify outlined a strategy to become an all-in-one media and AI powerhouse, centered on its "Large Taste Model" that processes 3.4 trillion daily taste signals. Key announcements included AI-generated personal podcasts, adaptive fitness-guided audio sessions, a "Reserved" feature for early concert ticket access, interactive podcast Q&A for Premium users, paid podcast memberships, expansion of its audiobook catalog to 700,000+ titles, and a rebuilt advertising infrastructure driving 68% year-over-year advertiser growth.
content: If you still think of Spotify as just a place to stream your favorite playlists, think again. At its 2026 Investor Day, the company dropped a massive hint about its future, making it clear they are officially moving past the "music app" label. Instead, Spotify is aiming for something much bigger: an all-in-one media and AI powerhouse fueled by hyper-personalization, creator tools, and your unique taste. At the center of that strategy is what Spotify calls its "Large Taste Model," an AI system trained on billions of user interactions across music, podcasts, and audiobooks. The company says it now processes roughly 3.4 trillion daily taste signals, data it wants to use to push listeners beyond passive consumption and toward more interactive experiences. The shift is already showing up in product announcements. Spotify introduced AI-generated "Personal Podcasts," audio programs built around user prompts and listening habits. It also previewed fitness-guided sessions that automatically adapt music tempo and coaching cues to match a workout pace. Another upcoming feature, called "Reserved," will allow top fans to access concert tickets before public sale through partnerships tied to artist engagement data. Podcasting remains a major part of the company's expansion plan, though Spotify now wants podcasts to behave more like interactive software than static audio files. Premium users in select markets can already ask questions about podcast episodes in real time while listening. The company is also introducing paid podcast memberships aimed at helping creators build recurring revenue streams directly inside Spotify's ecosystem. Audiobooks are getting similar treatment. Spotify said its audiobook catalog has grown to more than 700,000 titles across 22 markets, and the company plans to expand audiobook subscription tiers later this year. The less flashy part of the presentation may matter more financially. Spotify spent considerable time discussing its rebuilt advertising infrastructure, which the company says is now driving stronger advertiser growth and improved monetization. Executives said active advertisers rose 68% year over year, while biddable ad channels now account for more than a third of ad revenue. What stood out was how aggressively Spotify framed AI as part of the listening experience itself rather than a background recommendation engine. For years, streaming companies competed on catalog size and distribution. Spotify now appears to think the next phase depends on whether platforms can make media more adaptive, conversational, and personalized. That creates a different kind of competition because Spotify is no longer positioning itself only against music streaming services. It is edging toward territory occupied by AI assistants, creator platforms, live entertainment networks, and digital media companies all at once.

title: Flywheel: the new support program for startups support organizations
summary: Smart Capital launched Flywheel, a 7-year support program for startup support organizations in Tunisia, with two instruments: Deal (grant up to 200k TND for new coaching programs) and Sail (performance-based grants for existing structures).
content: During the official launch ceremony of the Anava fund of funds, Smart Capital unveiled a new support program for startups and support structures. Called Flywheel, this program will be spread over 7 years. Thus, two instruments will be addressed to support structures. The first instrument, called Deal, aims to support the launch of new coaching programs. To do this, it offers a grant worth 200 thousand dinars according to eligibility criteria that will assess the value proposition of this program, the key skills of the team, vision, structuring, cohesion, and connection with other structures and programs in the ecosystem. The second instrument, Sail, will allow existing coaching structures to benefit from a grant whose value is based on performance metrics such as the number of coached startups that have received the label, or the number of coached startups that have raised funds. The goal of this mechanism is to ensure the sustainability of these structures.

title: Un regard inédit sur les émeutes du pain de 1984 en Tunisie
summary: Près de quarante-deux ans après les émeutes du pain de janvier 1984, l'économiste Sadok Rouai publie un ouvrage intitulé « Tunisie - Émeutes du pain de janvier 1984 : Mythes et réalités sur le rôle du FMI et de la BCT ». Ancien cadre de la Banque centrale de Tunisie et du FMI, l'auteur propose une relecture documentée basée sur les archives officielles, analysant les mécanismes ayant conduit à la hausse des prix des produits subventionnés, le rôle des recommandations internationales et les arbitrages politiques de l'époque.
content: Près de quarante-deux ans après les émeutes du pain de janvier 1984, un nouvel ouvrage remet en perspective l'un des épisodes les plus marquants de l'histoire contemporaine tunisienne. Dans ''Tunisie - Émeutes du pain de janvier 1984 : Mythes et réalités sur le rôle du FMI et de la BCT'', l'économiste Sadok Rouai propose une relecture documentée de cette crise, fondée sur les archives officielles et son expérience au sein des grandes institutions financières internationales. Les événements de janvier 1984 restent associés à une explosion sociale déclenchée par la hausse des prix des produits de première nécessité, notamment le pain. Rapidement étendue à plusieurs régions du pays, la contestation avait pris une ampleur nationale avant d'être sévèrement réprimée, faisant plusieurs dizaines de morts et des centaines de blessés. Longtemps résumés à une réaction populaire face à une mesure économique impopulaire, ces événements font ici l'objet d'une relecture plus approfondie. Ancien cadre de la Banque centrale de Tunisie (BCT), Sadok Rouai est diplômé de la Faculté de droit et des sciences économiques de Tunis ainsi que de l'Institut technique des banques de Paris. Il a poursuivi sa carrière au Fonds monétaire international (FMI), où il a exercé pendant plus de trente ans comme conseiller principal. Fort de ce parcours, l'auteur confronte les récits politiques aux documents issus des archives du FMI et de la BCT. Il replace les événements dans le contexte économique du début des années 1980, marqué par les déséquilibres budgétaires, la pression de la dette extérieure et la mise en place, à l'instigation du FMI, des politiques d'ajustement structurel. Au fil des pages, l'ouvrage analyse les mécanismes ayant conduit à la hausse des prix des produits subventionnés. Il interroge le rôle des recommandations internationales, les marges de manœuvre des autorités tunisiennes et les arbitrages politiques opérés dans un contexte de forte contrainte financière. L'auteur examine également les alternatives économiques envisageables à l'époque ainsi que les effets durables de ces choix sur les politiques publiques du pays. Cette démarche vise à dépasser les lectures simplifiées de l'histoire pour mettre en lumière la complexité des interactions entre décisions nationales et contraintes internationales. Au-delà de la dimension historique, l'ouvrage ouvre une réflexion plus large sur les rapports entre souveraineté économique et institutions financières internationales. Dans un contexte mondial toujours marqué par les questions de dette et de réformes économiques, cette publication résonne avec des enjeux toujours actuels, notamment le refroidissement (sinon le gel) des liens entre le FMI et la Tunisie depuis l'accession de Kaïs Saïed à la présidence de la république. Ce dernier rejette les injonctions de l'institution financière internationale en matière de réformes politiques (levée progressive des subventions, cession partielle des entreprises publiques déficitaires, réduction de la masse salariale du secteur public...), en ayant sans doute présent à l'esprit les émeutes du pain de 1984, qui ont été provoquées par la hausse brutale des prix du pain et des pâtes alimentaires suite à la levée des subventions d'État décidée par le gouvernement Mohamed Mzali.

title: Apply for SLINGSHOT 2026 ($2M)
summary: SLINGSHOT 2026 invites deep tech startups globally to compete for over S$2.2 million in grant prizes and access to Singapore's innovation ecosystem. Applications close June 30, 2026.
content: SLINGSHOT 2026 is redefining the future as Singapore celebrates a decade of making the global homeground for innovation. The program invites deep tech startups with trailblazing innovations to compete and scale from Singapore's innovation arena - where you can experiment boldly, test fearlessly, and propel your business into the global spotlight. Take your startup to new heights by competing for a spot as one of SLINGSHOT 2026's Top 50 Global Startups! Gain access to Singapore's vibrant startup ecosystem and vie for a share of over S$2.2 million in grant prizes. Thematic Areas: Advanced Computing and Intelligence, Advanced Manufacturing and Industrial Systems, Digital Technologies and Securities, Environmental, Energy and Sustainability, Health and Biomedical. Benefits of Participation: Prize Grand Winner of SLINGSHOT 2025: S$400,000 Startup SG Grant Prize; 1st Runner Up: S$300,000 Startup SG Grant Prize; 2nd Runner Up: S$200,000 Startup SG Grant Prize. How to Apply: Ready to make Singapore your launchpad to the world? Click the SLINGSHOT 2026 portal to begin your application. The deadline for submission is Tuesday, June 30, 2026.

title: African Builder? Apply for Laptop LLM Challenge 2026 ($20k)
summary: Africa Deep Tech Challenge launches Laptop LLM Challenge 2026 with a $20k prize pool for innovators building efficient large language models that run on consumer-grade laptops, focusing on African innovation and accessible AI.
content:
Applications are now open for the Laptop LLM Challenge 2026, an initiative by the Africa Deep Tech Challenge that invites innovators, researchers, developers, and AI builders to create large language model (LLM) solutions that run efficiently on consumer-grade laptops. The challenge seeks practical approaches to developing and deploying AI models that can operate with limited computing resources while addressing real-world problems across Africa. By encouraging locally relevant AI innovation, the programme aims to support the development of accessible language technologies that can function without dependence on expensive infrastructure.
Benefits of Participation:
- Cash Prize Opportunities: Selected teams will compete for a share of the competition's prize pool, with top-performing solutions receiving financial rewards.
- Global Recognition: Participants will gain visibility among AI researchers, technology leaders, investors, and industry stakeholders.
- Opportunity to Showcase Technical Expertise: The challenge provides a platform to demonstrate ability to build resource-efficient language models.
- Contribution to African AI Development: Participants will develop solutions that address local challenges while contributing to Africa's AI ecosystem.
- Community and Ecosystem Engagement: The programme brings together researchers, developers, startups, students, and AI practitioners for collaboration and partnerships.
Eligibility: Open to individuals and teams with technical and research talent, with a focus on African innovation.
How to Apply: Applications close on Friday, July 24, 2026. Participants are encouraged to submit entries before the deadline and ensure solutions meet all technical and documentation requirements.

title: Top AI Stock & Forex Bots 2026: Tested & Ranked
summary: VentureBurn's VibeQuant team tested eight AI stock and forex trading bots over three weeks in June 2026, evaluating setup time, risk controls, interface clarity, and stability during volatile sessions. The ranking covers SaintQuant, Trade Ideas (Holly AI), 3Commas, MetaTrader 4/5, Forex Fury, and Tickeron, with SaintQuant ranked best overall for hands-off beginners due to no-code setup, built-in risk management, and multi-market support.
content: VibeQuant covers automated trading, quantitative strategy, and risk management, with a focus on cutting through hype toward realistic, evidence-based expectations. For this guide, VibeQuant set up and ran each bot reviewed - first on demo accounts, then with small live allocations - over a three-week period in June 2026, tracking setup time, risk-control behavior, and stability during volatile sessions. Areas of focus: automated trading systems, quantitative strategy design, risk management, forex and equity markets. Over three weeks in June 2026, our team ran eight AI stock and forex bots side by side - on demo accounts first, then small live allocations - to answer one question readers keep asking on Reddit and in our inbox: which ones actually work? This ranking is the result. No affiliate fluff, no invented profit screenshots - just what we saw when we set each bot up and let it trade. How We Tested These Trading Bots: Transparency first - here is exactly what we did, so you can weigh our verdict: Duration: 3 weeks (demo accounts, then small live allocations). What we measured: setup time, whether coding was required, how risk controls (stop-loss, position sizing) behaved, interface clarity, and stability during volatile sessions. Markets: major forex pairs (EUR/USD, GBP/USD) and large-cap US stocks. What we did not do: publish specific return figures. Short tests cannot prove profitability, and past results never guarantee future ones. We report process and behavior, not promises. A grounding point from our experience: the best automated trading bots are not fortune tellers. They win on discipline, speed, and risk management - not on predicting the market. Quick Comparison: Top AI Stock & Forex Bots 2026. 1. SaintQuant - Best Overall for Hands-Off Beginners: Best for traders who want automated stock and forex-style strategies without coding. SaintQuant was the fastest to get running in our test. Setup took minutes: we picked a pre-built strategy, set an allocation, and it began executing without any configuration or scripting. Risk controls like exposure limits and position sizing are built into each strategy rather than left to you, and during a volatile forex session the strategy scaled exposure down rather than doubling into the move. It runs 24/7 across crypto, stocks, and futures from one account. New users can test it with a $99 free trial credit plus a $7 registration bonus, with no deposit required. Pros: No-code, built-in risk management, multi-market, free trial. Cons: Deep manual customization is limited by design; returns are pursued, not guaranteed. 2. Trade Ideas (Holly AI) - Best for Stock Idea Generation: Best for active stock traders who want AI to surface setups. Trade Ideas' "Holly" scans the market and highlights candidates in real time. In testing, it was excellent at surfacing ideas quickly, but it is a discovery assistant, not a hands-off autopilot - you still make the decisions. The interface rewards experience and carries a learning curve. Pros: Powerful real-time scanning, strong for research. Cons: Steep learning curve; not truly hands-off; stocks only. 3. 3Commas - Best for Multi-Exchange Bot Trading: Best for users comfortable configuring their own bots. 3Commas offers grid, DCA, and other bot types across many exchanges. Setup took longer in our test because meaningful results depend on how you configure it - powerful, but not plug-and-play. Its risk behavior is only as good as your settings, so it rewards users willing to tune parameters. Other bots tested include MetaTrader 4/5 + EAs (Forex expert advisors), Forex Fury (Set-and-forget FX), and Tickeron (AI pattern signals).

title: 7 Tracks, 1 Future: AfriLabs Annual Gathering 2025 Sets The Stage For Africa's Innovation Renaissance
summary: AfriLabs announces its 10th Annual Gathering in Nairobi, Kenya (7-9 October 2025) under the theme "Africa's Innovation Future: Policy, Partnerships, and Progress." The event features seven tracks covering Policy & AI, Investment, Gender & Inclusion, Hub Solutions, Green & Blue Economy, Creative Digital Economy, and Makerspaces, aiming to drive actionable collaboration across Africa's innovation ecosystem.
content: The 10th edition of the AfriLabs Annual Gathering (AAG) is heading to Nairobi, Kenya from 7th to 9th October 2025, celebrating a decade of innovation and ecosystem growth across the continent. With the theme "Africa's Innovation Future: Policy, Partnerships, and Progress," this milestone event promises cutting-edge insights. More than just a conference, the 2025 Gathering presents a curated experience designed to deepen conversations and spur real action through seven transformative tracks: Policy, Data & AI in Africa; Investor & Investment; Gender and Inclusion; Hub Solution; Green and Blue Economy; Creative Digital Economy; and Makerspaces. title: 217+ startups, ₦11B fraud, and $3B valuations: Insights from the 2025 Nigeria Payments Report
summary: Zone and TechCabal Insights released the 2025 Nigeria Payments Report, highlighting explosive growth in e-payment channels (70% YoY transaction volume rise), regulatory momentum from CBN initiatives, cross-border expansion by fintechs like LemFi and Rise leveraging $20B+ remittances, and persistent challenges including infrastructure gaps, fraud threats, and a 54% dip in African fintech funding. Key players: Moniepoint adapted to stricter KYC, Flutterwave valued at $3B despite ₦11B fraud incident, Zone processes ₦100B monthly. VCs invested $140M in H1 2024. Future focus: AI fraud detection, open banking, AfCFTA cross-border payments, bridging funding gap.
content: Zone, a regulated blockchain-powered payment infrastructure company, has released the Nigeria Payments Report 2025 in partnership with TechCabal Insights. This second edition builds on the 2024 report which tracked how COVID-19 spurred a surge in digital payments, with e-transaction values doubling to over ₦1.25 quadrillion, and over 200 fintech startups accounting for 42% of all tech funding, driving financial inclusion to 76%. The 2025 report maps Nigeria's fast-evolving payment landscape, covering open banking, AI-powered fraud detection, and blockchain innovations. Standout players and milestones: Moniepoint adapted quickly to stricter KYC rules while deepening mobile money adoption. Flutterwave, valued at $3 billion, remained a market leader despite a major ₦11 billion fraud incident, underscoring industry-wide security concerns. Banks are deepening collaboration with fintechs on open banking and AI-driven fraud detection, while regulators like CBN drive innovation with diaspora accounts and real-time compliance checks. Despite a funding slowdown, VCs still bet big on Nigeria, injecting $140 million in H1 2024 alone. Key findings: Explosive growth: E-payment channels (POS, mobile money, USSD, RTGS) drive 70% year-on-year rise in transaction volumes, led by fintechs. Regulatory momentum: CBN initiatives including Payment Systems Vision (PSV), open banking guidelines, and new diaspora account categories (NRNOA/NRNIA) foster a more secure and inclusive ecosystem. Cross-border expansion: Nigerian fintechs such as LemFi and Rise leverage annual remittance flows of over $20 billion to scale Africa's global payments influence. Persistent challenges: Infrastructure gaps, fraud threats, and a 54% dip in overall African fintech funding underline the need for sustained investment. Zone's VP Olayiwola Osoba noted rapid collaboration among banks, fintechs, and regulators reshaping the economy, but bold moves in risk management and cross-border innovation are key. CEO Obi Emetarom stated Zone processes over ₦100 billion monthly, totaling more than ₦1 trillion annually, and targets 20x growth in 2025 following regulatory approval of its decentralised PTSA model. Next steps for 2025: Scaling AI-powered fraud detection and RegTech partnerships (e.g., SmileID, Dojah); using open banking and AfCFTA frameworks to grow cross-border payment solutions; bridging the fintech funding gap to solidify Nigeria's lead on the continent.

title: New GSMA report shows how Africa's top mobile operators are building AI language models in African languages
summary: The GSMA Mobile Economy Africa 2026 report reveals that six major African mobile operators (Airtel, Axian Telecom, Ethio Telecom, MTN, Orange, Vodacom) have partnered to build AI language models trained on African languages. The initiative has already produced a working Swahili reasoning model and aims to address barriers in data, compute, talent, and policy to unlock AI for Africa's 2,000+ languages.
content: GSMA published its Mobile Economy Africa 2026 report on Tuesday, and one section details how six of Africa's largest mobile network operators have partnered with the industry body to build artificial intelligence (AI) language models trained on African languages. The effort is aimed at closing a gap that leaves most of the continent unable to use mainstream AI tools in the languages people actually speak. Known as the G6, the operators - Airtel, Axian Telecom, Ethio Telecom, MTN, Orange, and Vodacom - first unveiled the initiative at Mobile World Congress (MWC) Kigali in October 2025, under the banner "AI language models in Africa, by Africa, for Africa." The collaboration brings together operators, researchers, startups, and civil society groups to tackle four barriers standing in the way of African-led AI development: data, compute, talent, and policy. The stakes are significant. Africa is home to more than 2,000 languages, accounting for over 30% of the world's total, yet large language models (LLMs) are trained mostly on English and other high-resource languages. That leaves hundreds of millions of people unable to use AI tools by voice or text in their own languages. The initiative has already produced a tangible result. At MWC Barcelona 2026, developers demonstrated the first open Swahili reasoning model, built in partnership with MeetKai Zambia. The model can browse and translate online content in Swahili, offering a template that could be replicated for other African languages. Closing this language gap is not only a social good, the report argues, it carries real economic weight. Operators that deploy AI effectively expand the addressable market for digital services to the hundreds of millions of Africans currently excluded by language barriers, helping push mobile's contribution to Africa's economy from $240 billion in 2025 to a projected $290 billion by 2030. Beyond the language models themselves, the report points to several pieces of supporting infrastructure falling into place. Cassava Technologies has opened an AI factory in South Africa, renting out GPU computing power and AI services to African developers, businesses, and mobile operators, while running a network-management system built on Nvidia's Blueprints framework to automate performance tuning across Africa's patchwork of network equipment. On skills, the MTN Skills Academy, a zero-rated platform open across 16 African markets, is training the workforce AI rollouts will need. On data, Google's WAXAL project gives that workforce something to build with. Released in February 2026 after three years of work with African universities, including Makerere University, the University of Ghana, and Addis Ababa University, the open dataset captures speech across 27 Sub-Saharan African languages spoken by more than 100 million people, designed to train voice AI and speech-recognition tools. Separately, the GSMA teamed up with Zindi, an African data science platform, to launch the African Trust & Safety LLM Challenge in March 2026. The result is a reusable safety benchmark that tests how AI models perform across more than 2,000 of the continent's languages.

title: Nigerian startup Data Entry Academy wins UNDP timbuktoo EdTech accelerator
summary: Lagos-based edtech platform Data Entry Academy won first place in the UNDP Get Ready 4 timbuktoo EdTech accelerator, securing a $10,000 prize after beating 1,429 applicants from across Africa. The startup, founded by Chioma Ifeanyi-Eze, topped finalists from Egypt and Senegal at a pitch event in Dakar. Another Nigerian startup, Varsity Scape, placed sixth among the top 10 winners.
content: Data Entry Academy, a Lagos-based edtech platform, has won first place in the United Nations Development Programme's (UNDP) Get Ready 4 timbuktoo EdTech accelerator, taking home a $10,000 prize after topping a field of 1,429 applicants from across Africa. The startup, founded by Chioma Ifeanyi-Eze, beat finalists from Egypt and Senegal during a July 1 pitch event in Dakar, Senegal. Another Nigerian startup, Varsity Scape, placed sixth among the top 10 winners. The award underscores the growing prominence of Nigeria's edtech ecosystem, particularly startups building digital skills and workforce development solutions, at a time when development finance institutions and ecosystem builders are expanding support for education technology across Africa. As venture funding becomes more selective, accelerator programmes such as timbuktoo and the Mastercard Foundation EdTech Fellowship are increasingly helping startups refine their products, access mentorship, and build investor readiness. According to UNDP, the 2026 Get Ready 4 timbuktoo EdTech accelerator attracted 1,429 applications from across Africa, with 1,099 startups meeting the eligibility criteria. Fifty startups were selected for the 12-week programme after nearly 2,850 blind evaluations by 19 independent experts. Twenty startups advanced to the final pitch, where 10 emerged as winners. Data Entry Academy took the top prize, followed by startups from Egypt and Senegal in second and third place, respectively. Founded in 2020, Data Entry Academy operates a 30-day online training programme that teaches workplace software skills, including spreadsheets, cloud accounting, invoicing, inventory management and payroll tools. The startup says it has trained more than 17,000 learners across Africa through courses delivered on Telegram and Teachable. Participants require only basic computer literacy to enrol, with learners ranging from job seekers and entrepreneurs to employees being upskilled by their organisations. Data Entry Academy and Varsity Scape previously participated in the Mastercard Foundation EdTech Fellowship, which provides selected startups with $100,000 in equity-free funding and advisory support. Data Entry Academy joined the programme's second cohort in 2024, while Varsity Scape participated in the third cohort. The award caps a 12-week accelerator in which startups received support to strengthen their business models, improve their products, develop market traction, and prepare for investment through the broader timbuktoo pipeline. The accelerator is part of UNDP's broader timbuktoo initiative, launched at the 2024 World Economic Forum in Davos with a goal of mobilising $1 billion over 10 years to support 10,000 startups and generate $10 billion in economic value across Africa. The initiative describes itself as a platform that brings together governments, investors, universities, and private sector organisations. It currently runs six pan-African thematic hubs, has trained 3,480 innovators, and operates 16 University Innovation Pods across the continent, with another 12 in the pipeline.

title: Nigeria sets October date for a digital postcode plan first proposed in 2009
summary: Nigeria will launch the first phase of a new digital postcode system in October, assigning every building a unique, machine-readable digital address tied to precise map coordinates. The National Digital Alphanumeric Postcode System aims to replace descriptive, landmark-based addresses that have complicated deliveries, identity verification, and emergency response. The plan, first proposed in 2009, has seen multiple false starts, but recent Federal Executive Council approval and validation exercises suggest this attempt may succeed, with adoption being the key challenge.
content:
Nigeria will begin the first phase of a new digital postcode system in October, assigning every building and location in the country a unique, machine-readable digital address tied to precise map coordinates in place of the descriptive, landmark-based addresses that have long complicated deliveries, identity verification, and emergency response. Bosun Tijani, Minister of Communications, Innovation and Digital Economy, disclosed the timeline at a workshop in Abuja organised with the Nigerian Postal Service (NIPOST), where officials and representatives of security and emergency response agencies discussed how the new postcode could become a shared framework for coordinating national response and verifying locations. The National Digital Alphanumeric Postcode System, Tijani wrote on X, is designed so that "every person, business, and location can be accurately identified and connected" within Nigeria's digital ecosystem. He described it as one of five defining initiatives from his three years in office, alongside Project BRIDGE, the country's 90,000-kilometre fibre rollout; the National Universal Communication Access Project, which is extending connectivity to more than 20 million Nigerians through 3,700 telecommunications towers; the Nigeria Data Exchange; and ongoing efforts to build out an artificial intelligence ecosystem.
Two decades of false starts: Nigeria has run a six-digit numeric postcode (Lagos Island's is 100001) since 1986, but missing street names and house numbers across much of the country have limited its usefulness. A plan to digitise it first surfaced in 2009, under the late President Umaru Musa Yar'Adua, before stalling amid policy and leadership changes. NIPOST tried smaller fixes: in 2017, it partnered with the British startup what3words to simplify location descriptions, and in 2018 launched two verification platforms, the Address Verification System and the Digital Addressing System, meant to build a live database of physical addresses. Neither solved the underlying problem, and by 2020, Google's Plus Codes had entered the Nigerian market with a similar workaround.
What is different this time: In March, the Federal Executive Council approved a Geographic Information System (GIS)-enabled version of the postcode, and NIPOST has spent recent months running a delineation model validation exercise, checking aerially mapped postcode boundaries against ground realities. October's rollout will cover a pilot group of states and local governments, not the whole country at once. NIPOST's Postmaster General and Chief Executive Officer, Tola Odeyemi, framed the bigger risk as adoption rather than engineering: implementation, not technology, will determine whether the system succeeds, she said at the workshop, adding that the goal now is to move from awareness to use.

title: 500 Global and Sanabil back eight startups in latest accelerator cohort
summary: 500 Global and Sanabil Investments (PIF-owned) announced the 11th cohort of the Sanabil Accelerator, selecting eight early-stage MENA startups from 690+ applications. The hybrid program ran April-July 2026, focusing on AI, fintech, healthcare, fraud prevention, insurtech, compliance, and digital content.
content: 500 Global and Sanabil Investments, a wholly owned company of Saudi Arabia's Public Investment Fund (PIF), have announced the 11th cohort of the Sanabil Accelerator by 500 Global, selecting eight early-stage startups from more than 690 applications. The accelerator ran from 5 April to 1 July 2026 and supports startups developing solutions across artificial intelligence, fintech, healthcare, fraud prevention, insurtech, compliance, and digital content, providing mentorship, operator-led workshops, investor exposure, and access to regional and global networks. The selected startups are Carevision, Emtethal, IBEA, Kami, Melon Digital, Raid AI, TPP, and Xsquare, with a strong focus on AI-enabled products and infrastructure technologies addressing regional challenges. The programme adopted a hybrid format, delivering its first two phases remotely in response to regional market conditions, while applications for the 12th cohort are now open to startups across the MENA region.

title: Agri Cash raises seed round to drive inclusive agri-fintech innovation in Egypt
summary: Agri Cash, an Egypt-based agri-fintech company, raises seed funding to scale operations and expand to new markets.
content: Agri Cash, the Egypt-based agri-fintech company transforming agricultural finance through digital innovation, has announced the successful close of its seed funding round. Alex Angels led the round, and a group of strategic investors also participated. While the investment amount remains undisclosed, the fresh capital will support Agri Cash's plans to scale its operations within Egypt and expand to new regional markets. Founded by Dr Diaa Youssef and Dr Mostafa El-Sehli, Agri Cash is on a mission to modernise and democratise access to financial services for Egypt's underserved farming community. The company offers an integrated platform that provides farmers not only with financing but also with AI-powered agronomic insights, zero-cost crop insurance, and tools to access local and global markets. With more than 1,500 active farmers onboarded and partnerships with 60 certified suppliers, Agri Cash has listed over 750 agricultural products on its platform. Through offline outreach efforts, the company has also reached 100,000+ farmers across rural Egypt, promoting trust and accelerating the digital transformation of the sector. Among its most innovative features is a Buy Now, Pay Later (BNPL) model tailored to the needs of agricultural and livestock producers. This Sharia-compliant offering allows farmers to access essential supplies interest-free for up to 12 months, with credit ceilings reaching EGP 3 million. Agri Cash is targeting EGP 500 million in business volume by the end of 2025 and plans to launch livestock financing products in 2026, further developing its end-to-end agricultural finance ecosystem. "This round marks a pivotal milestone in our journey to build a more inclusive, tech-enabled agricultural economy," said Dr Diaa Youssef, Co-Founder and CEO of Agri Cash. "We believe that empowering small and medium-scale farmers with the right tools and financial access will drive sustainable development across Egypt's agricultural value chain." Agri Cash's platform addresses persistent challenges in the farming sector, including complex loan procedures and the lack of Sharia-compliant financial alternatives. With this latest funding, the company is well-positioned to deepen its impact, improve productivity, and foster resilience across Egypt's agricultural landscape.

funding.investors: Alex Angels and strategic investors

title: Disrup Tech Ventures invests in Morocco's Chari to expand fintech inclusion across Africa
summary: Disrup Tech Ventures invests in Chari, a Y Combinator-backed startup that is transforming Morocco's retail sector into a nationwide fintech network.
content: Disrup Tech Ventures, a leading early-stage fintech venture capital fund, announces its investment in Chari, a Y Combinator-backed startup that is transforming Morocco's retail sector into a nationwide fintech network. This marks Disrup Tech's first investment in Morocco and its second in Africa outside of Egypt, underscoring the fund's growing commitment to supporting fintech innovation across the continent. Founded in 2020 by Ismael Belkhayat and Sophia Alj, Chari has built a licensed fintech platform that turns thousands of neighbourhood shops into access points for digital payments and financial services. By leveraging its payment institution licence from the Central Bank of Morocco, Chari is enabling merchants to function as financial hubs for their communities, giving consumers access to services that were previously out of reach. While its mobile app also allows retailers to order consumer goods from FMCG distributors with rapid delivery, Chari's mission goes far beyond e-commerce. The company is digitising financial flows in the informal economy, reducing dependency on cash, providing instant working capital, and embedding insurance and payment solutions directly into merchants' day-to-day operations. In doing so, Chari is building what could become the financial backbone of Morocco's informal retail economy. "Our investment in Chari is a milestone for Disrup Tech," said Mohamed Okasha, Managing Partner at Disrup Tech Ventures. "Chari is redefining how financial services are delivered at the grassroots level. By empowering small shops to act as financial gateways, Chari is creating the foundation for a new, inclusive fintech infrastructure in Morocco. This is exactly the kind of transformative model we seek to support across Africa." With over 20,000 retailers already onboarded, Chari is scaling rapidly to become the go-to platform for both commerce and financial services in Francophone Africa. "We are delighted to welcome Disrup Tech Ventures as a partner on our journey," said Ismael Belkhayat, CEO and Co-Founder of Chari. "With Disrup Tech's backing, we are accelerating our mission to turn every corner shop into a financial access point. Together, we will continue to build technologies that drive financial inclusion and economic growth across Africa." The investment forms part of Chari's Series A extension round, and as part of the deal, Disrup Tech Ventures will join Chari's board of directors, working closely with the founding team to advance its regional growth and fintech vision.

funding.investors: Disrup Tech Ventures

content: Trendle, the parent company of MASAHA App, has announced the successful closure of its second Seed investment round at a valuation of $1.5 million. The round was supervised by Dr. Yaqoub Al-Sane's Law Office and included participation from Fifty Studios Holding alongside a group of angel investors. MASAHA is the first technology platform dedicated to modernising and transforming the tailoring sector through automation and artificial intelligence solutions, bringing digital innovation to one of the region's most underserved traditional industries. The funding round formed a strategic alliance that combines technical expertise, marketing strength, and legal support. It was led by Fifty Studios Holding as a strategic technology partner, Waleed Al-Sulaim as a marketing partner and angel investor, in addition to Green Company represented by Mohammed Al-Fahad and angel investor Abdulmohsen Khaled Al-Sane. During Nexus Conference, MASAHA officially unveiled the fully redeveloped version of its application, introducing a significantly enhanced user experience alongside a roadmap of upcoming AI-powered solutions designed to elevate the tailoring ecosystem. Commenting on the round, Faisal Al-Qarnous, Co-founder and CEO of MASAHA, said that the investment marks a major turning point for the company, noting that its value goes beyond capital to validate a unified partnership model that brings together technology and operations. He added that the new partnerships will directly improve the user experience and overall service quality while thanking the strategic partners, angel investors, and Dr. Yaqoub Abdulmohsen Al-Sane & Partners Law Firm for their role in the round's success. MASAHA's upcoming plans include structured operational and geographic expansion, with GCC expansion in 2026-2027 positioned as a top priority.

title: Arabic AI startup Nanovate secures $1 million pre-seed to scale in GCC
summary: Nanovate, a Cairo-based AI company, has raised $1 million in a pre-seed funding round led by a group of angel investors.
content: Nanovate, a Cairo-based AI company, has raised $1 million in a pre-seed funding round led by a group of angel investors. Founded nine months ago by Nancy Madbouly and Ahmed Gamal, Nanovate specialises in Arabic-native AI solutions designed to help businesses operate faster, smarter, and in their own language. The company develops end-to-end Arabic AI technologies, including advanced voice and chat agents, automation systems, and customised AI applications. Nanovate is supported by MINT Incubator by EG Bank and Raya Future TECH Accelerator. The startup's mission is to bridge the gap between AI innovation and the Arabic-speaking world by offering technology that understands and reflects the region's linguistic diversity-covering 22 Arabic dialects. Nanovate has also launched the beta version of its AI dashboard, enabling companies to build their own Arabic chat agents without coding. The platform combines language understanding, real-time speech and emotion AI, and workflow automation into a unified ecosystem. "This isn't just another AI startup-it's a movement to put Arabic at the center of global innovation," said Ahmed Gamal, co-founder and CEO of Nanovate. "We're building technology that understands us, speaks like us, and works for our region. With this round, we're ready to take Arabic AI to a whole new level." Having developed its own LLM models, Nanovate is now poised to become a regional leader in Arabic AI infrastructure, creating a foundation for the next wave of intelligent, localised enterprise solutions.

funding.investors: group of angel investors

title: Lofty Inc Capital secures $43 million first close for its pan-African fund
summary: Lofty Inc Capital Management has secured a $43 million first close for its new Lofty Inc Alpha Fund, a late-seed investment vehicle aimed at scaling Africa's most promising tech startups.
content: Lofty Inc Capital Management has secured a $43 million first close for its new Lofty Inc Alpha Fund, a late-seed investment vehicle aimed at scaling Africa's most promising tech startups, particularly in Nigeria, Egypt, Kenya, and Francophone Africa. Lofty Inc is one of Africa's most established venture capital firms. With over a decade of investing experience, the firm has backed startups such as Flutterwave, Andela, Wave Mobile Money, Omni Retail, Relianc Health, Thndr, and Moove-which recently secured $100 million in investment from Uber and Mubadala. Lofty Inc has played a key role in shaping Africa's tech ecosystem while delivering strong returns to investors. The fund's first close attracted a mix of African and international backers, including sovereign wealth funds, development finance institutions (DFIs), US family offices, and regional investment bodies. Investors include Egypt's Micro, Small, and Medium Enterprises Development Agency (MSMEDA), Tunisia's Anava Fund of Funds, FMO (the Dutch Entrepreneurial Development Bank), Proparco with FISEA, the International Finance Corporation (IFC), Africa Grow (advised by DEG Impact Gmb H), the Dutch Good Growth Fund (DGGF), and US-based First Close Partners. The fund also secured commitments from African high-net-worth individuals (HNIs), reinforcing strong regional confidence in Lofty Inc's ability to back transformative companies. Lofty Inc's leadership team brings decades of investment experience and operational expertise across African and global markets. Idris Ayodeji Bello (Managing Partner) has invested in over 100 startups in 25 markets and has achieved 14 successful exits. Mariam Kamel (General Partner) brings investment banking and startup advisory experience from working with founders in the MENA region. Kevin Simmons (General Partner) has worked as a founder, operator, and investor across 15 markets spanning three continents. They lead a skilled investment and operations team across Africa's largest tech hubs, including Francophone Africa, ensuring strong local execution and founder support. The Alpha Fund is designed to help startups transition from early traction to scalable growth. Beyond capital, Lofty Inc leverages its network and expertise to support founders in navigating operational challenges, securing strategic partnerships, and accelerating market expansion. With increasing investor interest and a rapidly growing tech ecosystem, Africa is emerging as a major driver of global innovation. The Alpha Fund builds on Lofty Inc's legacy of identifying and supporting top-tier entrepreneurs, ensuring long-term value creation for startups and investors.

funding.investors: Lofty Inc Capital Management, Egypt’s Micro, Small, and Medium Enterprises Development Agency (MSMEDA), Tunisia’s Anava Fund of Funds, FMO, Proparco, the International Finance Corporation (IFC), Africa Grow, the Dutch Good Growth Fund (DGGF), and US-based First Close Partners

title: Morocco's Enakl raises $2.3 million to scale intelligent shared mobility
summary: Morocco's Enakl raises $2.3 million in Seed funding round
content: Startup ENAKL announces the closing of a $2.3 million Seed funding round, finalised in December 2025, following a first initial round of $1.4 million completed at the end of 2024. This new funding will enable Enakl to accelerate the deployment of its intelligent shared mobility solutions for companies, public-sector actors, and end users, while preparing the launch of its Saa S offering. The round brings together three new Moroccan investors: Azur Innovation Fund, Witamax and MFounders, alongside the reinvestment of two existing investors: Catalyst Fund and Digital Africa.

title: Egypt's i SUPPLY lands $3 million Sharia-compliant investment
summary: Egypt's i SUPPLY secures $3 million Sharia-compliant investment from Bokra to scale operations and expand access to medical supplies.
content: Bokra, the MENA region's first goal-driven digital investment platform, has announced a $3 million Sharia-compliant financing agreement with i SUPPLY, a leading digital platform for pharmaceutical and medical supply distribution, to scale its operations and expand access to medical supplies across Egypt. Through this collaboration, Bokra has structured a revenue-based revolving financing model that fully complies with Sharia principles. This funding will fuel i SUPPLY's expansion by enhancing its supply chain operations and improving service in underserved communities. Ayman El Sawy, Founder and CEO of Bokra, commented: "This partnership reflects Bokra's mission to unlock high-impact, Sharia-compliant investment opportunities that generate meaningful economic and social value. By extending a revenue-based revolving financing to i SUPPLY, we're supporting a data-driven healthcare disruptor to scale responsibly while offering our investors access to a real economy product that delivers purpose-driven returns. It's a clear example of how ethical finance can fuel growth in essential sectors and drive sustainable, long-term outcomes." With this financing, i SUPPLY will scale its operations, deepening its digital footprint across Egypt's pharmaceutical ecosystem. Leveraging artificial intelligence and predictive analytics, the platform streamlines procurement and optimises inventory, helping resolve long-standing inefficiencies in the healthcare supply chain. Ibrahim Emam, Co-founder and CEO of i SUPPLY, commented: "We're proud to partner with Bokra to accelerate our mission. Access to flexible, non-dilutive financing enables us to grow responsibly and stay laser-focused on what matters most, making access to medicine faster, more reliable, and powered by data." This partnership represents a shared commitment to innovation and impact, bringing together fintech and healthtech to advance a smarter, more equitable future for healthcare distribution in Egypt and beyond. It demonstrates the viability of revenue-based financing as a model for empowering SMEs and high-growth ventures across strategic sectors. This financing marks the next chapter in Bokra's strategy to expand its portfolio of Sharia-compliant, asset-backed offerings, following the successful EGP 3 billion sukuk issuance for Aman. It reflects our ongoing commitment to bridging ethical finance with real-world impact, empowering investors to achieve their goals through products rooted in purpose, performance, and inclusivity.

funding.investors: Bokra

title: Nabta Health closes $2 million pre-Series A to expand hybrid women's health model
summary: Nabta Health closes $2 million pre-Series A funding round to expand its hybrid women's health model.
content: Nabta Health ("NABTA") is pleased to announce the closing of a $2 million Pre-Series A funding round, bringing its total funding to date to US $4.5 million. The Pre-Series A funding will enable NABTA to accelerate its mission to deliver accessible, evidence-based women's healthcare and to expand its AI-powered hybrid model across the Middle East and Africa. The investment will be used to deepen partnerships with employers, insurers, and healthcare providers; introduce new diagnostic and at-home testing pathways; and strengthen NABTA's clinical infrastructure in collaboration with leading hospitals.

The round was led by a consortium of international and African investors, including Axian Group, reflecting the growing interest of regional investors in supporting homegrown cybersecurity solutions. Nucleon Security will use the new capital to scale its Zero Trust and agentic AI-powered platforms, which deliver continuous, proactive, and automated threat analysis and response. These solutions are already deployed with more than 100 clients worldwide. The company aims to strengthen its presence in Morocco, Tunisia, Benin, Togo, Côte d'Ivoire, and Madagascar, while also building an African cybersecurity ecosystem to share best practices and develop local solutions that can be exported globally.

title: Y Combinator leads Munify's $3 million seed round
summary: Cairo-based fintech Munify has raised $3 million in a seed round led by Y Combinator, with participation from BYLD and Digital Currency Group, alongside other strategic investors.
content: Cairo-based fintech Munify has raised $3 million in a seed round led by Y Combinator, with participation from BYLD and Digital Currency Group, alongside other strategic investors. The funding coincides with Munify's graduation from YC's Summer 2025 batch, marking one of the few MENA fintech startups to join the accelerator in a cycle dominated by AI companies. Founded in 2024 by Khalid Ashmawy, a former Microsoft and Uber executive and co-founder of proptech platform Huspy, Munify is building a cross-border digital bank tailored to Egyptians abroad. Its platform offers instant, low-cost remittances to Egypt, the ability to open U., S. bank accounts, issue debit cards, and tools to hedge against local currency volatility. Munify's services are already available in the U., S., U., K., Europe, and the GCC, targeting freelancers, SMEs, and Egyptians living overseas who face high remittance fees and delays in settlement. The new capital will be used to scale engineering and compliance teams, strengthen regulatory and banking partnerships, and expand into additional markets. "Remittance flows are one of the most critical financial lifelines for Egypt, yet millions still face costly, slow, and fragmented services," said Khalid Ashmawy, Founder and CEO of Munify. "We're building the infrastructure to make global banking and payments radically more accessible for Egyptians, wherever they live."

funding.investors: Y Combinator, BYLD, Digital Currency Group, and other strategic investors

title: Libya's Mataa closes seed round from local angel investors
summary: Mataa, a Libya-based e-commerce platform, announced the closing of its first Seed investment round, backed by a group of Libyan business angels.
content: Mataa, a Libya-based e-commerce platform, announced the closing of its first Seed investment round, backed by a group of Libyan business angels. The funding marks a significant milestone in the startup's journey to enhance digital commerce in Libya and the broader North African region. Speaking on the occasion, Mataa's Founder and CEO Ibrahim Shuwehdi stated that the funds will be used to strengthen operational capacity, particularly in first-mile and last-mile logistics, expand its central warehouse, and onboard new suppliers and product categories. The company also plans to recruit experienced regional talent in the e-commerce sector to support long-term growth. Mataa will now double down on its marketplace model, enabling Libyan merchants to reach over 6 million internet users nationwide. The platform also supports Facebook-based sellers by allowing seamless product integration, which can boost conversions and reduce paid advertising expenses. Shuwehdi emphasised that investor confidence in the founding team and Libya's central geographic location-at the heart of a 275 million-person, $830 billion-gross North African market-were key factors in attracting this round. The company views this round not just as a financial boost but as a signal to the wider ecosystem to encourage more venture investment in Libyan startups and SMEs.

funding.investors: Libyan business angels

title: Breadfast bags $10 million from EBRD, nears $400 million valuation
summary: Breadfast, Egypt's market-leading online grocery delivery platform, has raised $10 million in a Series B2 funding round from the European Bank for Reconstruction and Development (EBRD), in partnership with Novastar Ventures.
content: Breadfast, Egypt's market-leading online grocery delivery platform, has raised $10 million in a Series B2 funding round from the European Bank for Reconstruction and Development (EBRD), in partnership with Novastar Ventures. This latest funding places Breadfast's valuation at an impressive $382-400 million, underscoring strong investor confidence in its business model and growth potential. The investment will accelerate Breadfast's ambition to extend its fulfilment network, increasing capacity in core cities-Cairo, Giza, Alexandria, and Mansoura-and expanding into new markets. The success of Breadfast reflects its vertically integrated model, managing everything from quality control in baking to last-mile delivery, thus enabling rapid, reliable service. Now handling nearly 1 million orders per month for over 300,000 active users, with revenues surpassing $150 million in 2024, Breadfast is emerging not only as a quick-commerce leader but also as a growing fintech player through its Breadfast Pay offering.

investors: European Bank for Reconstruction and Development (EBRD)
European Bank for Reconstruction and Development (EBRD)

title: Morocco's VOVE ID secures fresh funds to support expansion ambitions
summary: Moroccan regtech startup VOVE ID has secured new investment from The Baobab Network to accelerate its growth across Africa and the Middle East.
content: Moroccan regtech startup VOVE ID has secured new investment from The Baobab Network to accelerate its growth across Africa and the Middle East. The funding (amount undisclosed) marks a key milestone for the high-end digital identity verification company, which launched earlier this year and has already gained rapid traction with its secure, AI-driven Know Your Customer (KYC) platform. Founded in 2024 by Khalid Aoussar, Tarik, and Youssef-alumni of Timeless Investment Gmb H, Remot Pass, and Pipedrive respectively-VOVE ID is aiming to transform how businesses verify identities in the $18.6 billion global KYC market. Its "verify once, use everywhere" approach allows users to reuse a verified digital identity across multiple platforms, simplifying onboarding while maintaining regulatory compliance. The platform supports features such as biometric authentication and automated document checks, already attracting clients across the MENA and African regions. The fresh capital will support VOVE ID's regional expansion, platform development, and team growth. As part of The Baobab Network's accelerator programme, VOVE ID will also gain strategic mentorship and access to a broader ecosystem to navigate regulatory challenges and scale faster. VOVE ID's core mission is to democratise access to enterprise-grade KYC tools for startups and SMEs often priced out of traditional solutions. With modular integration, pay-as-you-go pricing, and a user-friendly interface, the startup enables even the smallest businesses to implement robust verification systems without compromising on quality or compliance. "Having The Baobab Network on board is a game-changer," said co-founder and CEO, Aoussar. "We're building a future where verifying your identity is seamless, secure, and accessible-regardless of business size or geography." With new funding and growing momentum, VOVE ID is positioning itself as a vital player in the digital identity infrastructure for emerging markets.

funding.investors: The Baobab Network

title: Moroccan proptech startup Agenz raises $5 million oversubscribed seed round
summary: Moroccan proptech startup Agenz raises $5 million oversubscribed seed round
content: Agenz, a Moroccan proptech startup specialising in real estate data and transactions, has raised an oversubscribed $5 million Seed funding round led by BREEGA, Attijariwafa Ventures, and Saviu Ventures. The company plans to use the new funding to accelerate its expansion beyond property data and transaction services and into the financial infrastructure layer of real estate. Founded by Malik Belkeziz and Badr Belkeziz and later joined by Wassila Berrada and Ayyoub Mouadden, Agenz is building technology solutions aimed at improving transparency and efficiency across the real estate ecosystem. The company said it selected investors with deep operational expertise and experience in helping ambitious companies scale, as it enters its next phase of growth. Agenz remains focused on its long-term mission of building the operating system for the next generation of real estate, creating a market that is more transparent, efficient, and accessible for all stakeholders.

funding.investors: BREEGA, Attijariwafa Ventures, Saviu Ventures

title: enza secures $6.75 million seed to further expand in Africa
summary: enza, a leading innovator in African payment solutions, has secured a $6.75 million investment in a seed equity round co-led by Algebra Ventures and Quona Capital.
content: enza, a leading innovator in African payment solutions, announced today that it has secured a $6.75 million investment in a seed equity round co-led by Algebra Ventures and Quona Capital. This milestone marks the first external funding for enza and will accelerate the company's mission to "liberate the world of payments for more inclusive, opportunity-led commerce" across the African continent. enza's comprehensive payment solutions empower banks and fintechs to provide seamless, locally relevant payment capabilities, including cards, wallets, and real-time payments. The capital infusion will enable enza to strengthen its presence across key markets in Africa, fostering deeper partnerships and improving responsiveness to the localised needs of its growing client base. Founded by payments industry stalwarts with accomplished careers in both global and African payments, enza is harnessing this market experience, knowledge, and track record to unlock opportunities for its clients in Africa. The business has already demonstrated the ability to tackle local market problems with innovative payment services that can reduce the cost of digital transactions. "We are thrilled to partner with Algebra Ventures and Quona Capital, who share our vision of the potential to transform financial services in Africa through the digitisation of payments," said Hany Fekry, CEO of enza. "This investment is a strong endorsement of our team, our market-leading services, and our commitment to empowering banks and fintechs to drive financial inclusion across the continent." enza are liberating payments across the African continent with the mission to enable more inclusive, opportunity-led commerce. enza empowers African banks, fintechs, and financial institutions with market-leading capabilities and innovation to differentiate themselves in their market. The enza services reduce the cost of transacting, thereby enabling their clients to profitably serve larger target markets and establish the foundations of broader financial services relationships. The partnership with Algebra Ventures and Quona Capital reinforces enza's goal to address the challenges and opportunities presented by Africa's fast-growing, digitally connected population. With over 60 million underserved SMEs across the continent and a largely underbanked population, enza is already making a significant impact by enabling financial institutions to differentiate themselves and to profitably attract new customers with market-relevant products. The enza platform provides the foundation for these clients to then cross-sell other financial services products by leveraging the intelligence generated by the underlying data. Existing client use cases include the enablement of digital payment options such as domestic schemes, real-time payment services, mobile money, and BNPL, alongside the international payment schemes, unlocking value for enza's customers and Consumers and Businesses in multiple African countries. "enza is exceptionally well-positioned to transform Africa's payments ecosystem by delivering the essential infrastructure that banks and fintechs require for success," said Tarek Assaad, Managing Partner at Algebra Ventures. "The enza leadership team have an impressive track record of starting, growing and exiting fintech businesses across the continent. enza was uniquely capable of hitting the ground running as a pan-African payments player from the outset and is experiencing remarkable growth across its product offering. We are delighted to support the team on their exciting journey." "Quona is excited to support this experienced team on its journey to have a significant impact driving financial inclusion through seamless access to digital payments for banks, fintechs, and ultimately the SMEs they aim to serve," added Johan Bosini, partner at Quona Capital.

funding.investors: Algebra Ventures and Quona Capital

title: Hypeo Ai secures investment to simplify brand-creator collaborations
summary: Renew Capital invests in Hypeo Ai, a Morocco-based startup that streamlines influencer marketing through AI.
content: Renew Capital has announced an investment in Hypeo Ai, a Morocco-based startup that is streamlining influencer marketing through artificial intelligence. Hypeo Ai helps brands and creators across Africa and the Middle East run faster, more efficient influencer campaigns by eliminating the need for manual tracking and fragmented tools. The platform connects brands with both human and AI-powered creators in under 15 minutes, offering an end-to-end campaign workflow that includes smart matching, pricing insights, and real-time performance tracking. "Our region has no shortage of talent. What's been missing is smart infrastructure," said Meriam Bessa, co-founder and CEO of Hypeo Ai. "We're building tools that allow brands and creators to meet faster, match better, and work smarter with the power of AI." The company was founded by Bessa alongside Oussama Sekkat and Salah Eddine Mimouni. "Hypeo Ai is tackling a clear gap in how influencer marketing works in this region," said Nihal Grii, manager of investment ecosystem development at Renew Capital. "We see potential in their approach to simplifying the process for both brands and creators, and we're glad to support their next stage of growth."

funding.investors: Renew Capital

title: Moroccan superapp ORA Technologies secures $1.9 million pre-Series A

content: ORA Technologies becomes one of the most locally funded Moroccan startups and announces a $1.9 million Pre-Series A funding round led by Witamax & Azur Innovation Fund. Historically backed by local tycoons, ORA Technologies is welcoming for the very first time VCs in its cap table to reach $4.4 million in funding since the company's inception in 2023. This funding round demonstrates strong confidence from local investors in ORA's disruptive journey. In the boiling Moroccan tech ecosystem, ORA Technologies is well-known as the maverick startup aiming to tackle Morocco's digital and financial inclusion challenges, driving its vision of an e-Morocco for everyone. This investment will fuel the company's ambitious growth strategy, allowing it to scale Kooul, its fast-growing food delivery platform that has spread rapidly to 6 cities in only 5 months, and accelerate the newly launched ORA Cash, its transformative digital payment & free money transfer solution.
